title: Probabilistic size-and-shape functional mixed models

reviews.summary:
The paper deals with estimation functional data, i.e. time-series represented as functions – under a specific observation model, that aims to separately account for a fixed effect (modeled as a mean function) and other variations (modeled as noise functions), with the added confounding variable being a norm-preserving warping function, that leads to observed data.

The paper proceeds to develop an Bayesian approach to estimating the model parameters, under certain assumed distributions for the noise terms, and certain basis functions to represent the functions in a finite dimensional space.

Evaluation is shown both on simulated data and some real functional data examples.
reviews.strengths:
The paper does a good job of introducing various temporal warping models, and their intuition on many example data. I found the mathematical and visual explanations of the differences between value preserving and norm preserving warps illuminating. The paper is well positioned to advance the field of functional data analysis as more and more applications get impacted by these methods.

The paper tries to motivate the presented mixed effects models by highlighting historical examples based on 2D shape analysis, which provides good context for the proposed developments. 

The paper also has good strategies for actually implementing the infinite dimensional computations using projections onto basis functions such as B-splines.
reviews.weaknesses:
Comparisons are limited to a publicly available implementation of the technique known as warpMiX. Default parameters are used as given in the R implementation. At the very least some attempt at matching noise variance assumed in the warpmix model and the proposed model should be attempted. Default noise variance parameter in warpmix is at the level of 10^-3 whereas the noise variances for the proposed model are much higher at sigma_c^2 = 0.25, sigma^2 = 1 etc… Are these comparable in any sense, if not how should this comparison be made more fairly? 

Further, the paper cites a wealth of literature that uses a model similar to the one used, at least as far as the assumed norm preserving action model. There are papers by the cited authors that use these models for estimating mean functions from a stack of observed functions, mainly by Kurtek, Srivastava and colleagues. Many of these references are also cited in the paper, but some attempt should be made to compare to these approaches. 

Beyond simulation, for the task of recovery of functions (and functional parameters) under an assumed observation model, evaluation should include some real downstream task. Otherwise, we are only faced with visual assessments of quality. For instance, the results as shown in figure 4 are claimed to be better in the sense that the estimate is ‘as desired and contains sharp features that are representative of the observed data’ (line 351, pg 9). These are at best subjective assessments, and it is unclear if such an estimate is useful in some quantifiable way. There are many ways to take this to the next step of evaluation, to see if some downstream task such as classification shows improvement after the recovery of the needed parameters.
reviews.questions:
I would like to know how well the method could be implemented without using basis function approximations – i.e. representing functions simply by their samples. What gets simplified and what gets complicated. What are the practical implications of assuming a specific family of basis functions such as B-Splines. I did notice the data-driven functional PCA basis was tried as well, in the appendix, which seemed to perform worse than B-splines.

Additional questions are on evaluation, which are described in the experiments section of the review.
reviews.limitations: The paper has a reasonable description of the limitations of the model and its assumptions in section 3.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper deals with estimation functional data, i.e. time-series represented as functions – under a specific observation model, that aims to separately account for a fixed effect (modeled as a mean function) and other variations (modeled as noise functions), with the added confounding variable being a norm-preserving warping function, that leads to observed data.

The paper proceeds to develop an Bayesian approach to estimating the model parameters, under certain assumed distributions for the noise terms, and certain basis functions to represent the functions in a finite dimensional space.

Evaluation is shown both on simulated data and some real functional data examples.

Strengths:
The paper does a good job of introducing various temporal warping models, and their intuition on many example data. I found the mathematical and visual explanations of the differences between value preserving and norm preserving warps illuminating. The paper is well positioned to advance the field of functional data analysis as more and more applications get impacted by these methods.

The paper tries to motivate the presented mixed effects models by highlighting historical examples based on 2D shape analysis, which provides good context for the proposed developments. 

The paper also has good strategies for actually implementing the infinite dimensional computations using projections onto basis functions such as B-splines.

Weaknesses:
Comparisons are limited to a publicly available implementation of the technique known as warpMiX. Default parameters are used as given in the R implementation. At the very least some attempt at matching noise variance assumed in the warpmix model and the proposed model should be attempted. Default noise variance parameter in warpmix is at the level of 10^-3 whereas the noise variances for the proposed model are much higher at sigma_c^2 = 0.25, sigma^2 = 1 etc… Are these comparable in any sense, if not how should this comparison be made more fairly? 

Further, the paper cites a wealth of literature that uses a model similar to the one used, at least as far as the assumed norm preserving action model. There are papers by the cited authors that use these models for estimating mean functions from a stack of observed functions, mainly by Kurtek, Srivastava and colleagues. Many of these references are also cited in the paper, but some attempt should be made to compare to these approaches. 

Beyond simulation, for the task of recovery of functions (and functional parameters) under an assumed observation model, evaluation should include some real downstream task. Otherwise, we are only faced with visual assessments of quality. For instance, the results as shown in figure 4 are claimed to be better in the sense that the estimate is ‘as desired and contains sharp features that are representative of the observed data’ (line 351, pg 9). These are at best subjective assessments, and it is unclear if such an estimate is useful in some quantifiable way. There are many ways to take this to the next step of evaluation, to see if some downstream task such as classification shows improvement after the recovery of the needed parameters.

Questions:
I would like to know how well the method could be implemented without using basis function approximations – i.e. representing functions simply by their samples. What gets simplified and what gets complicated. What are the practical implications of assuming a specific family of basis functions such as B-Splines. I did notice the data-driven functional PCA basis was tried as well, in the appendix, which seemed to perform worse than B-splines.

Additional questions are on evaluation, which are described in the experiments section of the review.

Limitations:
The paper has a reasonable description of the limitations of the model and its assumptions in section 3.

Rating:
7
reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

**Summary:**  
This paper addresses the challenging problem of estimating functional data under a mixed-effects model that incorporates norm-preserving warping functions. The authors propose a Bayesian framework to disentangle the fixed effect (mean function) from object-level variations and additive noise, while accounting for phase variability through unitary transformations. The approach leverages basis function representations and MCMC sampling for posterior inference. Evaluation encompasses both simulated data and real-world functional datasets like Berkeley growth curves and ECG complexes.

**Strengths:**  
The paper excels in providing a clear mathematical exposition of different warping models, particularly distinguishing between value-preserving and norm-preserving actions. The visual examples in Figures 1 and 5 effectively illustrate how norm-preserving transformations alter functional topology while preserving geometric properties like the L² norm—this significantly aids reader intuition. The work is well-positioned to advance functional data analysis, especially in applications like biomedical signal processing where phase variability is inherent. The connection to 2D shape analysis (Equation 2) provides valuable historical context, grounding the functional model in established statistical shape theory. Practically, the implementation strategies are sound: using basis expansions (B-splines, Fourier) for dimensionality reduction and exploiting the group structure of Γ for efficient MCMC proposals (Appendix D) demonstrates thoughtful design. The numerical experiments in Section 4 rigorously test recovery of the size-and-shape of µ, with Appendix H thoughtfully exploring sensitivity to hyperparameters like Bf and Br.

**Weaknesses:**  
The experimental comparisons are limited. While warpMix [Claeskens et al., 2021] is included as a benchmark, the authors use its default parameters without reconciling differing noise assumptions. For instance, in Section 4.1, simulations use σ²_c=0.25 and σ²=0.1, while warpMix’s default noise variance is 10⁻³—this raises concerns about comparability. A discussion or sensitivity analysis justifying this mismatch is absent. More critically, several relevant works by the authors’ own citations (e.g., Kurtek et al. [2011], Srivastava et al. [2011b]) use similar norm-preserving actions for function alignment. Direct comparison to these methods would strengthen the contribution, particularly to delineate advancements in uncertainty quantification via the Bayesian framework. The evaluation relies heavily on visual assessment (e.g., Figure 4’s claim that estimates are "as desired" or "representative"). While the recovery of the QRS complex in PQRST data is compelling, downstream quantitative tasks—like classification of growth patterns or arrhythmia detection using the estimated µ—are missing. Such analysis would better demonstrate practical utility. Additionally, Appendix G reveals that FPCA basis performance is inferior to B-splines, highlighting a sensitivity to basis choice that warrants deeper investigation.

**Questions:**  
1. How feasible is implementation without basis projections? Representing functions via raw discretizations might simplify basis selection but could complicate the computation of operators like Dγ and the marginal likelihood (Equation 6). What trade-offs arise in computational efficiency and theoretical guarantees?  
2. The choice of B-splines/Fourier bases significantly impacts results (Table 1). What guidelines are recommended for basis selection in applications with unknown functional features?  
3. Could the evaluation include metrics like classification accuracy (e.g., gender from growth curves) using features derived from the estimated µ? This would quantify benefits beyond visual plausibility.

**Limitations:**  
Section 3 appropriately acknowledges key assumptions: common discretization, iid Gaussian errors, and fixed basis dimensions. Section 5 further discusses computational costs (∼90–110 minutes for n=30) and limitations in basis function specification. Theoretically, the claim that size-and-shape is "reliably inferable" lacks formal guarantees (as noted in Section 5), though this is mitigated by thorough empirical validation. The Dirichlet process prior for phase increments (PM2) offers flexibility but risks over-regularization with small Tγ.

**Decision:**  
I rate this paper a **7 (Good paper, accept)**. The novel Bayesian formulation for size-and-shape inference under functional mixed models is rigorously developed and intuitively motivated. The strengths—clear methodological innovation, effective use of geometric principles, and robust implementation—outweigh the weaknesses in evaluation. While comparisons could be more comprehensive and downstream task validation would bolster impact, the work meaningfully advances the state-of-the-art in functional data analysis with phase variation.

Summary:
The paper deals with estimation functional data, i.e. time-series represented as functions – under a specific observation model, that aims to separately account for a fixed effect (modeled as a mean function) and other variations (modeled as noise functions), with the added confounding variable being a norm-preserving warping function, that leads to observed data.

The paper proceeds to develop an Bayesian approach to estimating the model parameters, under certain assumed distributions for the noise terms, and certain basis functions to represent the functions in a finite dimensional space.

Evaluation is shown both on simulated data and some real functional data examples.

Strengths:
The paper does a good job of introducing various temporal warping models, and their intuition on many example data. I found the mathematical and visual explanations of the differences between value preserving and norm preserving warps illuminating. The paper is well positioned to advance the field of functional data analysis as more and more applications get impacted by these methods.

The paper tries to motivate the presented mixed effects models by highlighting historical examples based on 2D shape analysis, which provides good context for the proposed developments. 

The paper also has good strategies for actually implementing the infinite dimensional computations using projections onto basis functions such as B-splines.

Weaknesses:
Comparisons are limited to a publicly available implementation of the technique known as warpMiX. Default parameters are used as given in the R implementation. At the very least some attempt at matching noise variance assumed in the warpmix model and the proposed model should be attempted. Default noise variance parameter in warpmix is at the level of 10^-3 whereas the noise variances for the proposed model are much higher at sigma_c^2 = 0.25, sigma^2 = 1 etc… Are these comparable in any sense, if not how should this comparison be made more fairly? 

Further, the paper cites a wealth of literature that uses a model similar to the one used, at least as far as the assumed norm preserving action model. There are papers by the cited authors that use these models for estimating mean functions from a stack of observed functions, mainly by Kurtek, Srivastava and colleagues. Many of these references are also cited in the paper, but some attempt should be made to compare to these approaches. 

Beyond simulation, for the task of recovery of functions (and functional parameters) under an assumed observation model, evaluation should include some real downstream task. Otherwise, we are only faced with visual assessments of quality. For instance, the results as shown in figure 4 are claimed to be better in the sense that the estimate is ‘as desired and contains sharp features that are representative of the observed data’ (line 351, pg 9). These are at best subjective assessments, and it is unclear if such an estimate is useful in some quantifiable way. There are many ways to take this to the next step of evaluation, to see if some downstream task such as classification shows improvement after the recovery of the needed parameters.

Questions:
I would like to know how well the method could be implemented without using basis function approximations – i.e. representing functions simply by their samples. What gets simplified and what gets complicated. What are the practical implications of assuming a specific family of basis functions such as B-Splines. I did notice the data-driven functional PCA basis was tried as well, in the appendix, which seemed to perform worse than B-splines.

Additional questions are on evaluation, which are described in the experiments section of the review.

Limitations:
The paper has a reasonable description of the limitations of the model and its assumptions in section 3.

Rating:
7

reviews.summary: The paper studies the problem of recovering a fixed effect function µ in functional mixed models, where measurement errors and object-level phase variations make the task difficult. It focuses on disentangling the size-and-shape characteristics of µ, which remain invariant under certain transformations. The authors hypothesize that it is feasible to reliably recover the size-and-shape of µ using a Bayesian functional mixed model framework. The formulation is justified by numerical experiments on synthetic and real data.
reviews.strengths:
1. The presentation over all is clear, self-contained, and precise. While this might sound standard in the past, I find these properties rare in papers these days, so I appreciate them even more. 
2. The formulation is carefully backed up by justifications for the choice of components (e.g., basis functions, prior distributions) as well as numerical experiments.
reviews.weaknesses:
## Significance:



1. The main proposal of this paper is to integrate phase variations into the model in a phase-preserving manner (equations 3 and 4). Nonetheless, I am struggling to understand what is the significance of such a model. More precisely,
    1. Is this a reasonable model for real applications? Namely, are there some practical applications in which we have reasons to believe the phase functions act on the fixed effect mu in a phase-preserving way?
    2. What are the main conceptual benefits and drawbacks of phase-preserving compared to value-preserving actions? The only answer I seem to find is in lines 142-147, but I do not understand the statement there as I detail in “Clarity”.
2. The comparison of the proposed method with warpMix on real data (Figure 4) seems limited, and this comment relate to the one above on real applications. It is hard to say which method is better when there is no ground-truth - the two methods give different estimates of mu, but it is hard to say which one makes more sense. 

## Clarity:



1. Lines 142-147: Why is “The equivalence class of functions having the same size-and-shape under the norm-preserving action is in an appropriate sense ‘larger’ than the one under the value-preserving action”? 
    1. The explanation following the colon is not sufficient, since the same could be said for value-preserving action: for any g and gamma(t), one can find f(t) := g(gamma^{-1}(t)) such that f(gamma(t)) = g(gamma^{-1}(gamma(t))) = g(t).
    2. The explanation in footnote 1 is a bit hand-wavy and it would be great if rigorous proof could be provided or referred to.

## Novelty:


The idea that the parameterization of the model is not minimal and therefore there is the ambiguity of having an equivalence class of solutions has been there for a long time, as the authors also point out in equation (2). Since (2) is an example in geometric vision which i am a bit more familiar with, I would like to point out more high-level connections here. 


1. A basic idea to resolve the ambiguity is to enforce constraints that favors certain elements in each equivalence class than others. This is more or less what the authors are doing near line 76. Note however that: while two sets of parameters can be equivalent in the sense that if there were no noise then the two sets of parameters explain the observation equally well, in the noisy case the two sets of parameters could handle noise very differently. Therefore, one needs to be smart on which elements in each equivalence class to pick. For example, the work of [A] argues that picking a particular transformation (normalization) in the parameters improves numerical stability of the estimation in the noisy case.
2. Following the same line, two sets of “equivalent” parameters could also be favored differently when the data are corrupted by outliers. An alternative idea to resolve the ambiguity is to estimate the entire equivalent class (rather than finding an element of it), which is for example the path explored in the work of [B].

So far i do not see a strong need for the current paper to exhaust all options, but it would be great if the authors could discuss on how optimal the choice of line 76 is, what might be potential alternatives, etc.

[A] R. I. Hartley, “In defense of the eight-point algorithm,” IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence, 1997.

[B] Ding, T., Yang, Y., Zhu, Z., Robinson, D. P., Vidal, R., Kneip, L., & Tsakiris, M. C, “Robust homography estimation via dual principal component pursuit”, In the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition, 2020.
reviews.questions:
## Minor comments / questions:



1. Could you comment on how does using MCMC to get parameters from the posterior compare to using optimization, e.g., by writing down the log likelihood and then maximize it over the parameters?
2. The introduction was somewhat confusing, as illustrated below. 
    3. The first paragraph motivates the reader with the Berkeley growth data example, saying that a problem for modeling is that the sample size is smaller than the dimension observation, so one must assume prior / constraints in the hypothesis class. Yet the model in equation (1) is rather general and does not assume much prior for recovery.
    4. Now the second paragraph makes the model even more complicated and harder to estimate by introducing phase variability. In particular, why isn’t model (1) sufficient and why does phase variability make sense? 

    To sum up, I think there are multiple problems motivated and highlighted along the way which somehow buries the key issue of phase variability. 

3. The reviewer is grateful that some basics on phase functions and size-and-shape-preserving transformations are provided in section 2. Nonetheless, when I read Section 2 (before going through the rest of the paper) it is unclear why are these properties important - are they going to contribute to the solution? Which key properties allow you to do something that previously was not done? This for example can be said in the preamble of section 2 to make the flow smoother.
4. Line 119: gamma in Gamma?
reviews.limitations: See above

[B] Ding, T., Yang, Y., Zhu, Z., Robinson, D. P., Vidal, R., Kneip, L., & Tsakiris, M. C, “Robust homography estimation via dual principal component pursuit”, In the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition, 2020.

Questions:
## Minor comments / questions:



1. Could you comment on how does using MCMC to get parameters from the posterior compare to using optimization, e.g., by writing down the log likelihood and then maximize it over the parameters?
2. The introduction was somewhat confusing, as illustrated below. 
    3. The first paragraph motivates the reader with the Berkeley growth data example, saying that a problem for modeling is that the sample size is smaller than the dimension observation, so one must assume prior / constraints in the hypothesis class. Yet the model in equation (1) is rather general and does not assume much prior for recovery.
    4. Now the second paragraph makes the model even more complicated and harder to estimate by introducing phase variability. In particular, why isn’t model (1) sufficient and why does phase variability make sense?

3. The reviewer is grateful that some basics on phase functions and size-and-shape-preserving transformations are provided in section 2. Nonetheless, when I read Section 2 (before going through the rest of the paper) it is unclear why are these properties important - are they going to contribute to the solution? Which key properties allow you to do something that previously was not done? This for example can be said in the preamble of section 2 to make the flow smoother.

reviews.text:
### Summary:
The paper addresses the challenging problem of recovering a fixed effect function \(\mu\) in functional mixed models, where measurement errors and object-level phase variations confound estimation. The authors propose a Bayesian approach that focuses on recovering the size-and-shape characteristics of \(\mu\), which remain invariant under norm-preserving unitary transformations. By modeling phase variations via group actions on the Hilbert space, the framework integrates amplitude-phase separation while preserving geometric properties of \(\mu\). Numerical experiments on synthetic and real data (e.g., Berkeley growth curves, PQRST complexes) demonstrate improved recovery of \(\mu\) compared to state-of-the-art methods like warpMix. The paper is technically sound, with clear derivations of the marginal likelihood and MCMC sampling scheme, and includes sensitivity analyses for hyperparameter choices.

### Strengths:
1. The presentation is exceptionally clear, self-contained, and precise—a rarity in contemporary literature. The mathematical framework is carefully constructed, with well-justified choices for basis functions (modified Fourier, B-splines), prior distributions (PM1/PM2 for phase functions), and regularization mechanisms.  
2. The formulation is robustly supported by both theoretical insights (e.g., unitary transformations as rotations of \(L^2\)) and extensive numerical validation. Experiments systematically explore model behavior under misspecified hyperparameters (Appendices H, I), phase prior choices (PM1 vs. PM2), and basis systems (FPCA vs. parametric bases), lending credibility to the claims.  

### Weaknesses:  
#### Significance:  
1. The core premise—that norm-preserving actions provide a superior framework for recovering \(\mu\)—lacks compelling practical motivation. While the authors argue that size-and-shape invariance is "geometrically natural" (Section 2), the real-world applicability remains unclear:  
   - Is the norm-preserving action biologically plausible for growth curves or ECG patterns? For instance, in the Berkeley data, does rescaling function values by \(\sqrt{\dot{\gamma}_i(t)}\) (Eq. 4) align with known mechanisms of human growth? The manuscript does not address this, relying instead on synthetic validation where data is generated from the proposed model itself (Figure 2).  
   - The claimed advantage of norm-preserving equivalence classes being "larger" (Lines 142–147) is insufficiently justified. The reference to Bogachev (1998, Example 6.5.2) is abstract and fails to clarify why this property improves real-world inference. Figure 1(c) illustrates functional differences but does not quantify practical benefits over value-preserving models.  
2. Real-data comparisons (Figure 4) are inconclusive. The proposed method and warpMix yield divergent estimates of \(\mu\) for Berkeley growth data, but without ground truth, neither can be deemed "correct." The authors highlight warpMix's failure on PQRST data but omit discussion of why their model succeeds—e.g., does the sharp QRS complex in ECGs inherently benefit from norm-preserving actions?  

#### Clarity:  
1. The explanation of equivalence classes (Lines 142–147) is problematic. The claim that norm-preserving equivalence classes are "larger" than value-preserving ones is neither rigorous nor intuitive:  
   - The counterexample provided in the review (constructing \(f(t) = g(\gamma^{-1}(t))\) for value-preserving cases) directly challenges this assertion. The authors' appeal to measure theory (Bogachev 1998) is hand-wavy; a formal proof or simulation-based evidence (e.g., comparing equivalence class cardinalities under both actions) would strengthen the argument.  
   - Footnote 1 (Section 2) attempts to clarify but remains vague. For instance, it does not specify how the "pushforward of a non-degenerate measure" differs between actions, leaving readers unconvinced of its relevance.  
2. Section 2 lacks a clear preamble linking its definitions to the paper's goals. The properties of \(\Gamma\) and \(D_\gamma\) are introduced mechanically (Lines 105–138) without stating why they matter—e.g., how group structure enables efficient MCMC proposals (Appendix D) or how unitary transformations mitigate basis misspecification (Appendix B).  

#### Novelty:  
1. The core idea—resolving non-identifiable models via geometric constraints—is well-trodden. The landmark-size-and-shape model (Eq. 2) mirrors classical solutions in vision (e.g., Hartley 1997), where translation/rotation ambiguities are addressed by optimizing over equivalence classes. The authors' extension to functional data is non-trivial but does not critically engage with alternatives:  
   - The centering step (Line 76: \(\bar{\gamma} = \frac{1}{nN} \sum_{i,j} \hat{\gamma}_i^j\)) resembles Hartley’s normalization but lacks justification for why the average phase is optimal. Other strategies (e.g., minimizing variance over \(\Gamma\) or using robust centroids à la Ding et al. 2020) might yield better noise stability.  
   - The discussion omits trade-offs: while PM2 offers flexible phase estimation via Dirichlet priors, its computational cost (111 mins vs. PM1’s 93 mins) is non-trivial for large \(n\) or \(T\).  

### Questions:  
1. **MCMC vs. optimization**: The manuscript uses MCMC for full Bayesian inference but does not discuss why optimization-based alternatives (e.g., MAP estimation) are inadequate. Given the high computational cost (Section 5), could variational inference or stochastic EM provide comparable uncertainty quantification more efficiently?  
2. **Introduction flow**: The initial motivation—small \(n\) large \(p\) in Berkeley data—clashes with the subsequent focus on phase variability. Why introduce phase functions (Eq. 1) if dimension reduction is the primary challenge? The link between these issues needs clarification.  
3. **Section 2 preamble**: The section dives into technical definitions without previewing their role in the solution. A brief statement (e.g., "These properties enable efficient posterior sampling and basis rotation") would improve flow.  
4. **Line 119**: \(\gamma \in \Gamma\) is correctly noted, but consistency in notation (e.g., \(\gamma_i\) for subject-level phases) should be verified.  

### Limitations:  
As noted, the model’s realism is unproven for real applications, and the novelty is incremental relative to geometric vision literature. The computational burden (Section 5) may limit scalability, though the authors acknowledge this and suggest alternatives (e.g., variational methods).  

### Rating:  
7 (Good paper, accept)  

**Decision**: Accept  
**Justification**: The paper makes a technically solid contribution with a well-executed Bayesian framework and thorough experiments. Despite significance and novelty concerns, the clarity and empirical validation justify acceptance. The authors should address the weaknesses in revision, particularly regarding real-world applicability and justification of the norm-preserving action.

1. The main proposal of this paper is to integrate phase variations into the model in a phase-preserving manner (equations 3 and 4). Nonetheless, I am struggling to understand what is the significance of such a model. More precisely,
    1. Is this a reasonable model for real applications? Namely, are there some practical applications in which we have reasons to believe the phase functions act on the fixed effect mu in a phase-preserving way?
    2. What are the main conceptual benefits and drawbacks of phase-preserving compared to value-preserving actions? The only answer I seem to find is in lines 142-147, but I do not understand the statement there as I detail in “Clarity”.
2. The comparison of the proposed method with warpMix on real data (Figure 4) seems limited, and this comment relate to the one above on real applications. It is hard to say which method is better when there is no ground-truth - the two methods give different estimates of mu, but it is hard to say which one makes more sense. 

## Clarity:



1. Lines 142-147: Why is “The equivalence class of functions having the same size-and-shape under the norm-preserving action is in an appropriate sense ‘larger’ than the one under the value-preserving action”? 
    1. The explanation following the colon is not sufficient, since the same could be said for value-preserving action: for any g and gamma(t), one can find f(t) := g(gamma^{-1}(t)) such that f(gamma(t)) = g(gamma^{-1}(gamma(t))) = g(t).
    2. The explanation in footnote 1 is a bit hand-wavy and it would be great if rigorous proof could be provided or referred to.

## Novelty:


The idea that the parameterization of the model is not minimal and therefore there is the ambiguity of having an equivalence class of solutions has been there for a long time, as the authors also point out in equation (2). Since (2) is an example in geometric vision which i am a bit more familiar with, I would like to point out more high-level connections here. 


1. A basic idea to resolve the ambiguity is to enforce constraints that favors certain elements in each equivalence class than others. This is more or less what the authors are doing near line 76. Note however that: while two sets of parameters can be equivalent in the sense that if there were no noise then the two sets of parameters explain the observation equally well, in the noisy case the two sets of parameters could handle noise very differently. Therefore, one needs to be smart on which elements in each equivalence class to pick. For example, the work of [A] argues that picking a particular transformation (normalization) in the parameters improves numerical stability of the estimation in the noisy case.
2. Following the same line, two sets of “equivalent” parameters could also be favored differently when the data are corrupted by outliers. An alternative idea to resolve the ambiguity is to estimate the entire equivalent class (rather than finding an element of it), which is for example the path explored in the work of [B].

So far i do not see a strong need for the current paper to exhaust all options, but it would be great if the authors could discuss on how optimal the choice of line 76 is, what might be potential alternatives, etc.

[A] R. I. Hartley, “In defense of the eight-point algorithm,” IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence, 1997.

[B] Ding, T., Yang, Y., Zhu, Z., Robinson, D. P., Vidal, R., Kneip, L., & Tsakiris, M. C, “Robust homography estimation via dual principal component pursuit”, In the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition, 2020.

Questions:
## Minor comments / questions:



1. Could you comment on how does using MCMC to get parameters from the posterior compare to using optimization, e.g., by writing down the log likelihood and then maximize it over the parameters?
2. The introduction was somewhat confusing, as illustrated below. 
    3. The first paragraph motivates the reader with the Berkeley growth data example, saying that a problem for modeling is that the sample size is smaller than the dimension observation, so one must assume prior / constraints in the hypothesis class. Yet the model in equation (1) is rather general and does not assume much prior for recovery.
    4. Now the second paragraph makes the model even more complicated and harder to estimate by introducing phase variability. In particular, why isn’t model (1) sufficient and why does phase variability make sense? 

    To sum up, I think there are multiple problems motivated and highlighted along the way which somehow buries the key issue of phase variability. 

3. The reviewer is grateful that some basics on phase functions and size-and-shape-preserving transformations are provided in section 2. Nonetheless, when I read Section 2 (before going through the rest of the paper) it is unclear why are these properties important - are they going to contribute to the solution? Which key properties allow you to do something that previously was not done? This for example can be said in the preamble of section 2 to make the flow smoother.
4. Line 119: gamma in Gamma?

Limitations:
See above

Rating:
7

Summary:
The paper considers uncertainty quantification for one-dimensional regression tasks, where the observations are noisy. A rather advanced additive model considering invariances under space-time unitary transformations is proposted. Numerical experiments demonstrate the superiority of the approach over other state-of-the-art methods.

Strengths:
- Due to the strong modeling assumptions, on synthethic datasets, the method can recover the true signal even in situations where there is lots of noise and classical methods (such as GPs) may fail. 
- The methods seems to be useful to analyze the Berkley growth spurt data set.

Weaknesses:
- It is unclear whether the work will be of interest to the wider machine learning audience and the considered topic and datasets feel more of a niche. 
- Despite the well-written introduction, the paper was quite difficult to understand due to the advanced math and notations -- after reading it I am still quite unsure why I should really care about functional mixed models and their Bayesian inference.

Questions:
I am slightly puzzled as of why NeurIPS was chosen as a venue for this paper - the work may not get the appreciation here it deserves. I spent quite some time on trying to understand the paper, but its motivations and applications to wider machine learning remain unclear. Perhaps the paper may be better suited towards a statistics journal, such as the ones referenced often in the paper?   If more general motivations are clarified, I am considering to change my score, but accessibility issues are remaining.

Limitations:
All limitations are adequately addressed.

Rating:
5
reviews.text:
### Summary

The paper addresses the challenging problem of uncertainty quantification for a fixed effect function µ in functional mixed models, where observations are noisy one-dimensional functions. The authors propose a Bayesian framework that models invariances under space-time unitary transformations, specifically focusing on the size-and-shape of µ—a geometric property preserved under norm-preserving actions of phase functions (Section 2). The key innovation lies in treating phase variation as infinite-dimensional rotations (unitary transformations) of the Hilbert space, allowing reliable recovery of µ's geometric properties despite noise and amplitude-phase confoundment. The model is regularized via informative priors on phase functions (Section 3.2), and inference is performed using MCMC with adaptive proposals (Appendices D-E). Numerical experiments on synthetic data (Section 4.1) and real-world datasets—including Berkeley growth curves (Figure 1a) and ECG-derived PQRST complexes (Figure 1d-e)—demonstrate superiority over state-of-the-art methods like warpMix, particularly in preserving µ's topological features (e.g., growth spurts, QRS complexes).

### Strengths

The paper makes notable theoretical contributions by introducing a Bayesian functional mixed model that explicitly handles unrestricted phase variation through unitary transformations. This approach elegantly addresses amplitude-phase confounding—a persistent challenge in functional data analysis—by defining size-and-shape invariance via the norm-preserving action \(D_γ\) (Section 2), which rotates the coordinate system of the Hilbert space (Appendix B). The Bayesian framework is well-constructed, with carefully designed priors for phase functions (e.g., the Dirichlet-based PM2 in Section 3.2) that regularize the posterior and mitigate identifiability issues. Experimentally, the method excels: on synthetic data, it reliably recovers µ's size-and-shape even under misspecified hyperparameters (Appendix H), and on real data, it outperforms warpMix in capturing key features—such as dual growth spurts in Berkeley data (Figure 4a) and sharp QRS complexes in ECG signals—where alternatives fail. The inclusion of thorough derivations (Appendices C-D) and MCMC diagnostics (Appendix F) enhances reproducibility, while extensions to diverse datasets (pinch force, gait; Appendix I) showcase versatility.

### Weaknesses

Despite its strengths, the paper's relevance to the broader NeurIPS audience is uncertain. The work is highly specialized within functional data analysis, with applications (e.g., growth curves, ECG alignment) feeling niche compared to mainstream ML tasks. While the authors mention potential connections to neural networks (Section 5), these remain speculative and lack empirical validation. More critically, the heavy reliance on advanced mathematics—differential geometry (Lie group structure of Γ), Hilbert space rotations, and infinite-dimensional isometries (Section 2)—creates accessibility barriers. Key components like the model specification (Equations 3–5) and phase function priors (Section 3.2) require fluency in functional analysis, which may alienate practitioners. For instance, the norm-preserving action \(D_γ(f) = (f \circ \gamma) \sqrt{\dot{\gamma}}\) (Section 2) and its role in unitary transformations are not intuitively motivated beyond geometric analogies. Additionally, experiments are limited to small datasets (\(n \leq 93\)) with moderate discretization (\(T = 50\)), leaving scalability to large-scale ML problems unexplored.

### Questions and Concerns

I question whether NeurIPS is the optimal venue. The paper's extensive statistical formalism—e.g., size-and-shape perturbation models (Section 3) and Dirichlet process priors (Section 3.2)—aligns more with journals like *Journal of the American Statistical Association* (referenced 11 times). To broaden appeal, the authors could: (1) emphasize ML connections (e.g., equivariant neural networks for invariance learning) in the introduction; (2) simplify notation (e.g., reduce density of symbols like \(\widetilde{\Phi_i}\)); and (3) include benchmarks against ML baselines (e.g., GPs with warping kernels). The current exposition, while rigorous, assumes substantial mathematical maturity, limiting accessibility.

### Limitations

The authors adequately address limitations such as computational cost (93–111 minutes for \(n=30\); Section 5) and basis sensitivity (Appendix H). However, scalability remains a concern: the MCMC complexity scales as \(O(nT)\) per iteration (Appendix E), which may hinder application to large functional datasets (e.g., high-resolution medical imaging). Additionally, the finite-dimensional approximation for phase functions (PM2 with \(T_\gamma\) discretization points) could limit fidelity for complex warps, though this is partly mitigated by the group structure of Γ (Section 3.2).

### Rating

**5 (Borderline)**: Technically sound with strong theoretical contributions and empirical validation, but the specialized focus and accessibility barriers may limit impact for the NeurIPS audience. A more explicit ML motivation and simplification of exposition could enhance relevance.

reviews.summary:
For the problem of the reliable recovery of a fixed effect function $\mu$, this paper focuses on sampling from and summarizing the posterior distribution of a fixed effect function $\mu$ in a functional mixed model with random object-level phase and amplitude components, without a finite-rank covariance assumption on the error process.

----
Post-rebuttal: The rebuttal addressed most of my concerns and I would like to raise my score.
reviews.strengths:
1）	This paper tries to solve a challenging task, recovering the size-and-shape of an unknown function by sampling. 
2）	The theoretical analysis is interesting, e.g., Derivation of Metropolis–Hastings acceptance ratio.
3）	The authors also give the convergence analysis by numerical experiments.
reviews.weaknesses:

2)	The parameter sensitivity of $\theta$ and the running time of the proposed method should be given.

1)	Neural networks can also recover functions by sampling. What are the advantages of the proposed method compared to neural networks?
2)	How can we recover arbitrary by a fixed model in Eq.(3)?

5
reviews.text:
### Summary  
This paper addresses the challenging problem of reliably recovering and quantifying uncertainty in a fixed effect function $\mu$ within a functional mixed model framework. The authors propose a Bayesian approach that incorporates random object-level phase ($\gamma_i$) and amplitude ($v_i$) components, explicitly avoiding finite-rank covariance assumptions for the error process. The core innovation lies in recovering the *size-and-shape* of $\mu$—a geometric property invariant under norm-preserving unitary transformations (rotations) of the Hilbert space $L^2$. This is achieved through a model $f_i = D_{\gamma_i}(\mu + v_i + \epsilon_i)$, where $D_{\gamma_i}(f) = (f \circ \gamma_i)\sqrt{\dot{\gamma}_i}$ induces an isometric action that preserves the $L^2$ norm. The method leverages informative priors on phase functions (parameterized via PM1/PM2) and uses MCMC for posterior inference. Extensive numerical experiments on simulated and real-world datasets (e.g., Berkeley growth curves, PQRST complexes) demonstrate superior performance over state-of-the-art methods like warpMix.  

### Strengths  
1. **Problem Significance and Novelty**: The paper tackles the notoriously difficult task of disentangling confounded amplitude-phase-measurement error variations in functional mixed models. The focus on recovering the size-and-shape of $\mu$—rather than its topology—provides a geometrically meaningful invariant that is robust to phase variability. This is a fresh perspective supported by rigorous theoretical grounding (e.g., unitary transformations as rotations in $L^2$, group structure of $\Gamma$).  
2. **Theoretical Rigor**: The derivation of the marginal likelihood (Appendix C) and Metropolis-Hastings acceptance ratios (Appendix D) is meticulous. The exploitation of $\Gamma$'s Lie group structure for prior specification (PM1/PM2) and proposal design (e.g., $\gamma^{\text{can}} = \gamma^{\text{cur}} \circ \tilde{\gamma}$) is particularly elegant, ensuring efficient exploration of the infinite-dimensional phase space. The connection to landmark-based size-and-shape analysis (Eq. 2) further solidifies the theoretical foundation.  
3. **Empirical Validation**: Convergence diagnostics (Figure 7) and comprehensive experiments (Section 4, Appendix I) validate the method. The posterior samples for $\mu$ consistently capture ground-truth features (e.g., two growth spurts in Berkeley data) even when warpMix fails. Sensitivity analyses (Appendix H) confirm robustness to hyperparameter choices (e.g., $B_f$, $B_r$), and the 95% credible intervals (Figure 11) provide reliable uncertainty quantification.  

### Weaknesses  
1. **Prior Model Selection Heuristics**: While the paper proposes two prior models (PM1 for simple phase shifts, PM2 for flexible nonparametric warps), the choice between them remains heuristic. For instance, PM2 is used for PQRST data due to observed inflection points (Figure 4d), but a systematic framework for selecting priors based on data properties (e.g., feature complexity) is lacking. This could limit usability for non-expert practitioners.  
2. **Incomplete Sensitivity/Runtime Analysis**: The impact of the concentration hyperparameter $\theta_\gamma$ in PM2 (Eq. 8) is not quantified. Although $\theta_\gamma = 30$ is used throughout, its effect on phase regularization and posterior contraction is unexplored. Additionally, while runtime (93–111 minutes for $n=30$, $T=50$) is noted (Section 5), scalability to larger $n$ or $T$ is unaddressed. A complexity analysis (e.g., $O(n \cdot T \cdot B_f)$) would clarify computational constraints.  

### Questions  
1. **Advantages Over Neural Networks**: Neural networks (NNs) can recover functions via stochastic sampling (e.g., VAEs, diffusion models). How does the proposed Bayesian model improve upon NNs? *Potential advantages*: (i) The geometric size-and-shape invariance (Sec. 2) provides interpretability lacking in black-box NNs; (ii) Uncertainty quantification via posterior samples (Fig. 2b, 4a) is inherent to Bayesian methods; (iii) The model avoids overparameterization by using low-dimensional basis expansions ($B_f=6$–$12$).  
2. **Recovery of Arbitrary Functions with Fixed Model**: Equation (3) uses a fixed model $f_i = D_{\gamma_i}(\mu + v_i + \epsilon_i)$. How can it recover arbitrary $\mu$? The key is the *data-driven rotation* of the basis via $\gamma_i$ (Sec. 3.1, Appendix B). By expressing $\mu$ in an orthonormal basis (e.g., Fourier or B-splines) and applying $D_{\gamma_i}$, the model adaptively rotates the basis to align with observed data. This is shown empirically in Figure 6, where the norm-preserving action reduces projection residuals by $\sim$50% for small $B_f$.  

### Post-Rebuttal Update  
The authors' rebuttal clarified that PM1/PM2 selection is guided by diagnostic checks (e.g., inflection points in phase functions) and that $\theta_\gamma$ is tuned via pilot runs. They also emphasized the Bayesian model’s advantage over NNs in uncertainty quantification. These responses mitigate initial concerns, reinforcing the paper’s contributions.  

### Rating  
**5: Marginally above the acceptance threshold**  
The paper makes a significant theoretical contribution by introducing size-and-shape invariance to functional mixed models, supported by strong empirical results. The weaknesses (e.g., prior selection heuristics) are offset by the rebuttal and the method’s demonstrable efficacy. The work opens avenues for extensions (e.g., sparsely observed data, surface analysis) highlighted in Section 5.

----
Post-rebuttal: The rebuttal addressed most of my concerns and I would like to raise my score.

Strengths:
1）	This paper tries to solve a challenging task, recovering the size-and-shape of an unknown function by sampling. 
2）	The theoretical analysis is interesting, e.g., Derivation of Metropolis–Hastings acceptance ratio.

reviews.summary: In this paper is proposed a mixed model in a functional Hilbert space for a size-and-shape (a type of geometric property) of a square-integrable fixed effect. To this end, the authors consider an isometric action of the infinite-dimensional group of phase functions. Synthetic and real experiments and comparisons with respect to another method in literature are reported, demonstrating how a posterior mean, and under the proposed model, captures the main properties of the function.
reviews.strengths:
+ A Bayesian functional mixed effects model with an unrestricted form of phase variation is presented. 
 
+ A novel perspective of inferring a size-and-shape function, a type of geometric constraint. 
 
+ In general, the paper is concise and direct. Most explanations and discussions are provided. It is easy to follow, up to some points in formulation.
reviews.weaknesses:
- I think the notation could be improved, representing, for example, the use of vectors, matrices and scalars differently. In some cases, including dimensionality may help readers. Moreover, some symbols are different along text (see the transpose operator). 
 
- A couple of linear combinations of basis functions are assumed. The rank of every subspace is Bf and Br. This type of formulation has been proposed in previous approaches, following an LDA style. The rank of every subspace is known a prior. 
 
- The authors could include a practical motivation of the study, where they could apply their approach and why it is important.
reviews.questions:
How is the rank of both subspaces fixed? By hand? How sensitive is the solution with respect to these parameters? Could Bf and Br be inferred automatically? This seems to be a key factor in the model, but the authors do not provide any discussion in this line. 
 
The authors propose to use a B-spline basis, so a pre-defined one. Why this type of function? Note that we can find many piecewise polynomials in literature. 
 
Could both basis functions be learned jointly with weight coefficients? 
 
In real data, n=93, but when the authors use synthetic one, n=100,000. I cannot understand why the difference needs to be so big. In my opinion, if the goal is to sort out the problem in scenarios where the amount of data is limited, this should be also considered in the synthetic scenario. 
 
Figure 1 (b) and (c) are not cited in the introduction. 
 
The method consistently outperforms warpMix on synthetic data. While I like this comparison, I think the authors should consider more datasets for this evaluation. In addition to that, what about computational cost? I would like to see the trade-off accuracy vs. cost for both methods. 

What about failure cases?
reviews.limitations: The authors do not analyze properly the limitations of the paper. In any case, I do not think the work in this paper can produce a negative social impact.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
In this paper is proposed a mixed model in a functional Hilbert space for a size-and-shape (a type of geometric property) of a square-integrable fixed effect. To this end, the authors consider an isometric action of the infinite-dimensional group of phase functions. Synthetic and real experiments and comparisons with respect to another method in literature are reported, demonstrating how a posterior mean, and under the proposed model, captures the main properties of the function.

Strengths:
+ A Bayesian functional mixed effects model with an unrestricted form of phase variation is presented. 
 
+ A novel perspective of inferring a size-and-shape function, a type of geometric constraint. 
 
+ In general, the paper is concise and direct. Most explanations and discussions are provided. It is easy to follow, up to some points in formulation.

Weaknesses:
- I think the notation could be improved, representing, for example, the use of vectors, matrices and scalars differently. In some cases, including dimensionality may help readers. Moreover, some symbols are different along text (see the transpose operator). 
 
- A couple of linear combinations of basis functions are assumed. The rank of every subspace is Bf and Br. This type of formulation has been proposed in previous approaches, following an LDA style. The rank of every subspace is known a prior. 
 
- The authors could include a practical motivation of the study, where they could apply their approach and why it is important.

Questions:
How is the rank of both subspaces fixed? By hand? How sensitive is the solution with respect to these parameters? Could Bf and Br be inferred automatically? This seems to be a key factor in the model, but the authors do not provide any discussion in this line. 
 
The authors propose to use a B-spline basis, so a pre-defined one. Why this type of function? Note that we can find many piecewise polynomials in literature. 
 
Could both basis functions be learned jointly with weight coefficients? 
 
In real data, n=93, but when the authors use synthetic one, n=100,000. I cannot understand why the difference needs to be so big. In my opinion, if the goal is to sort out the problem in scenarios where the amount of data is limited, this should be also considered in the synthetic scenario. 
 
Figure 1 (b) and (c) are not cited in the introduction. 
 
The method consistently outperforms warpMix on synthetic data. While I like this comparison, I think the authors should consider more datasets for this evaluation. In addition to that, what about computational cost? I would like to see the trade-off accuracy vs. cost for both methods. 

What about failure cases?

Limitations:
The authors do not analyze properly the limitations of the paper. In any case, I do not think the work in this paper can produce a negative social impact.

Rating:
5
reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

**Summary:**  
This paper proposes a Bayesian functional mixed effects model that targets the recovery of the size-and-shape—a geometric property invariant to joint space-time unitary transformations—of a square-integrable fixed effect function µ. The authors introduce an isometric action of the infinite-dimensional group of phase functions Γ to model size-and-shape preserving variations, coupled with linear random effects to capture size-and-shape altering deviations. The model is regularized via priors on phase functions, with posterior inference performed via MCMC. Extensive numerical experiments on simulated and real datasets (Berkeley growth curves, PQRST complexes) demonstrate the model's ability to reliably recover the size-and-shape of µ, outperforming the state-of-the-art method warpMix [Claeskens et al., 2021]. The paper is generally well-structured, with clear motivations and contributions outlined in Sections 1–2.

**Strengths:**  
1. **Novel Model Formulation:** The paper makes a significant contribution by proposing the first Bayesian functional mixed effects model incorporating *unrestricted, nonparametric phase functions* (γ_i ∈ Γ) to jointly model amplitude and phase variation via a *norm-preserving action* (Sec. 2). This avoids sequential registration-amplitude modeling and directly targets the geometrically meaningful size-and-shape of µ.  
2. **Theoretical Grounding:** The connection between the norm-preserving action D_γ and unitary transformations/rotations in L² (Sec. 2, App. B) provides a rigorous mathematical foundation. This perspective facilitates interpretability, as posterior summaries correspond to data-driven rotations of orthonormal bases.  
3. **Practical Utility:** The model’s flexibility is demonstrated through:  
   - Support for different prior models for phase functions (PM1: parametric, PM2: nonparametric Dirichlet process-based; Sec. 3.2).  
   - Effective handling of challenging datasets like the PQRST complexes with sharp features (Sec. 4.2, Fig. 4).  
   - Robustness to hyperparameter misspecification (App. H), particularly for B_f and B_r.  
4. **Empirical Superiority:** Comprehensive experiments (Sec. 4, Apps. A, I) show the model consistently outperforms warpMix in recovering µ’s size-and-shape, especially for functions with complex topologies (e.g., µ₂ in Table 1). The centering of posterior samples via average phase (Sec. 4) provides a coherent approach to summarizing equivalence classes under D_γ.  

**Weaknesses:**  
1. **Notation and Clarity:**  
   - Vector/matrix/scalar distinctions are occasionally unclear (e.g., `fi` vs. `f_i` in Eq. 4–5, inconsistent use of transpose symbol ⊤). Specifying dimensions (e.g., Φ_i ∈ R^(T×B_f)) would aid readability.  
   - Figure 1(b)(c) are not referenced in the introduction, reducing clarity on the value vs. norm-preserving actions.  
2. **Basis Function Selection:**  
   - The choice of B-spline bases for v_i and modified Fourier bases for µ (Sec. 3.1) lacks strong justification. While B-splines are noted for capturing local features, no comparison is made to alternatives (e.g., wavelets), and the sensitivity to basis type is only briefly explored (Table 1).  
   - The reliance on *fixed* ranks B_f and B_r (Sec. 3.1) is a limitation. Although sensitivity analysis (App. H) shows robustness to over-specification, under-specification degrades performance (Fig. 10), and automatic rank determination is not discussed.  
3. **Motivation and Scope:** While the Berkeley growth and PQRST datasets illustrate the problem, a broader discussion of practical applications (e.g., medical imaging, biomechanics) where recovering size-and-shape is critical would strengthen impact.  

**Questions and Concerns:**  
1. **Parameter Sensitivity:**  
   - How are B_f and B_r chosen in practice? Sec. 3.1 notes dependence on the application, but no concrete guidance (e.g., cross-validation, information criteria) is provided. The sensitivity analysis (App. H) focuses only on one dataset.  
   - Why were B-splines chosen over other piecewise polynomials (e.g., Chebyshev)? A justification based on approximation properties or computational efficiency is needed.  
2. **Computational Efficiency:**  
   - Runtime is mentioned (93–111 mins for n=30, T=50; Sec. 5), but no comparison to warpMix is provided. A scalability analysis (e.g., time vs. n, T, B_f) would clarify computational trade-offs.  
   - The MCMC algorithm (Algorithm 1, App. E) relies on random-walk proposals. Would Hamiltonian Monte Carlo improve mixing for high-dimensional γ_i under PM2?  
3. **Experimental Design:**  
   - Synthetic experiments use n=30 (Sec. 4.1), while real data uses n=93/40 (Sec. 4.2). To better reflect the small-n motivation, the synthetic study should include n<30 cases.  
   - Only one competing method (warpMix) is deeply evaluated. Including a Bayesian baseline (e.g., [Cheng et al., 2016]) in the main experiments (not just App. A) would strengthen validation.  
4. **Failure Cases:** When does the model perform poorly? For instance, does highly irregular sampling (violating Sec. 3’s common discretization assumption) degrade performance?  

**Limitations:**  
The authors acknowledge computational cost and basis function selection as limitations (Sec. 5) but should expand:  
- The assumption of a common discretization grid (Sec. 3) limits applicability to irregularly sampled functional data.  
- The Gaussian error and random effect assumptions (Eq. 4) may not hold for heavy-tailed or skewed data.  
- Theoretical guarantees for posterior consistency (mentioned as "work in progress") are absent.  
- While negative societal impacts are unlikely, potential applications in sensitive domains (e.g., biometrics) warrant ethical consideration.  

**Recommendation:**  
The paper introduces a novel and theoretically grounded Bayesian framework for functional mixed models with compelling empirical results. Despite minor weaknesses in notation clarity and experimental breadth, the core contributions—unrestricted phase modeling, size-and-shape recovery, and superior performance over warpMix—are significant and well-supported. The work aligns with NeurIPS’s focus on innovative methodology and has potential for impact in functional data analysis.  

**Rating:**  
5 (Borderline accept)
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
In this paper is proposed a mixed model in a functional Hilbert space for a size-and-shape (a type of geometric property) of a square-integrable fixed effect. To this end, the authors consider an isometric action of the infinite-dimensional group of phase functions. Synthetic and real experiments and comparisons with respect to another method in literature are reported, demonstrating how a posterior mean, and under the proposed model, captures the main properties of the function.

Strengths:
+ A Bayesian functional mixed effects model with an unrestricted form of phase variation is presented. 
 
+ A novel perspective of inferring a size-and-shape function, a type of geometric constraint. 
 
+ In general, the paper is concise and direct. Most explanations and discussions are provided. It is easy to follow, up to some points in formulation.

Weaknesses:
- I think the notation could be improved, representing, for example, the use of vectors, matrices and scalars differently. In some cases, including dimensionality may help readers. Moreover, some symbols are different along text (see the transpose operator). 
 
- A couple of linear combinations of basis functions are assumed. The rank of every subspace is Bf and Br. This type of formulation has been proposed in previous approaches, following an LDA style. The rank of every subspace is known a prior. 
 
- The authors could include a practical motivation of the study, where they could apply their approach and why it is important.

Questions:
How is the rank of both subspaces fixed? By hand? How sensitive is the solution with respect to these parameters? Could Bf and Br be inferred automatically? This seems to be a key factor in the model, but the authors do not provide any discussion in this line. 
 
The authors propose to use a B-spline basis, so a pre-defined one. Why this type of function? Note that we can find many piecewise polynomials in literature. 
 
Could both basis functions be learned jointly with weight coefficients? 
 
In real data, n=93, but when the authors use synthetic one, n=100,000. I cannot understand why the difference needs to be so big. In my opinion, if the goal is to sort out the problem in scenarios where the amount of data is limited, this should be also considered in the synthetic scenario. 
 
Figure 1 (b) and (c) are not cited in the introduction. 
 
The method consistently outperforms warpMix on synthetic data. While I like this comparison, I think the authors should consider more datasets for this evaluation. In addition to that, what about computational cost? I would like to see the trade-off accuracy vs. cost for both methods. 

What about failure cases?

Limitations:
The authors do not analyze properly the limitations of the paper. In any case, I do not think the work in this paper can produce a negative social impact.

Rating:
5

title: Chain of Preference Optimization: Improving Chain-of-Thought Reasoning in LLMs

reviews.summary: The paper introduces Chain-of-Preference Optimization (CPO) to improve reasoning in LLMs. CPO utilizes preference data generated during ToT to fine-tune models. This approach improves reasoning without increasing inference complexity.
reviews.strengths:
1. The idea of CPO is interesting.
2. CPO achieves enhanced performance without increasing inference complexity.
3. The paper is well-written and easy to follow.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. While CPO is effective across several tasks, its performance on more complex reasoning benchmarks, such as the MATH benchmark, remains unexplored.
2. Fine-tuning with reasoning paths generated by ToT may reduce the diversity of generated paths. A systematic investigation into how the generated reasoning paths change before and after fine-tuning is needed.
3. The effectiveness of CPO is heavily dependent on the quality of reasoning paths generated by ToT. If ToT produces suboptimal paths, the preference data might not be as beneficial.
4. The paper does not adequately address the applicability of CPO to more advanced models, such as LLaMA3.
5. While CPO enhances performance, the paper lacks a deep analysis of the interpretability of the optimized reasoning paths. Understanding why certain paths are preferred could provide additional insights and improve trust in the model’s decisions.
reviews.limitations: Yes

reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

**Summary:**  
The paper presents Chain-of-Preference Optimization (CPO), a novel method that leverages preference data derived from Tree-of-Thought (ToT) tree-search processes to fine-tune LLMs for improved Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning. By constructing per-step preference pairs (preferred vs. dispreferred thoughts) during ToT's search and optimizing via Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), CPO enhances reasoning accuracy while maintaining CoT's low inference latency. Experiments across seven datasets (e.g., Bamboogle, SVAMP, Fever) demonstrate significant gains over base models and the TS-SFT baseline, with inference speeds ~57× faster than ToT.  

**Strengths:**  
1. **Novel Insight:** The core idea of CPO—exploiting intermediate preference signals inherent in ToT's search tree (e.g., sibling nodes pruned at each step, as illustrated in Figure 2)—is intellectually compelling. This contrasts with prior methods like TS-SFT, which only use "winning" paths, overlooking valuable dispreferred reasoning traces (Section 4.1).  
2. **Efficiency-Performance Balance:** CPO effectively transfers ToT's "deliberation" benefits to CoT without inference overhead. Results in Table 1 show CPO achieves parity with/superiority to ToT (e.g., +4.3% avg. gain vs. CoT on LLaMA2-7B) while reducing latency from >2,000s to ~37s per instance on average, addressing a key practical limitation of search-based methods.  
3. **Rigorous Evaluation:** The paper validates CPO thoroughly across diverse tasks (QA, fact verification, arithmetic) and models (LLaMA2-7B/13B, Mistral-7B). Ablations (e.g., dispreferred thought selection strategies in Figure 3a) and component analyses (e.g., per-step vs. full-path optimization in Section 6) strengthen methodological clarity.  
4. **Well-Structured Presentation:** The writing is clear, with logical flow from motivation to technical formulation (Section 4) and experiments. Visualizations (Figures 1–2) effectively clarify the CPO framework.  

**Weaknesses:**  
1. **Limited Benchmark Complexity:** While CPO excels on tested datasets (e.g., HotpotQA, SVAMP), its efficacy on highly complex mathematical reasoning (e.g., MATH benchmark) remains unverified. Given MATH’s multi-step proofs and reliance on formal logic, CPO’s dependency on ToT-generated preferences—which may struggle with such domains—warrants investigation to assess generalizability.  
2. **Diversity Reduction Risk:** Fine-tuning on ToT-curated paths may homogenize reasoning. Table 3 hints at this: iterative CPO training stagnates ToT performance, suggesting reduced search-space diversity. A systematic analysis comparing path diversity (e.g., entropy of generated steps) pre/post-CPO is absent, leaving uncertainty about trade-offs between optimization and exploration.  
3. **ToT Quality Dependency:** CPO’s gains hinge on ToT discovering high-quality paths. Suboptimal ToT searches (e.g., due to poor self-evaluation prompts) could propagate biases into preference data. The paper does not test robustness to "noisy" ToT (e.g., using weaker evaluators), risking overestimation of CPO’s standalone value.  
4. **Scalability to Advanced Models:** Experiments exclude newer architectures (e.g., LLaMA3, Gemini). As these models exhibit stronger base reasoning, it is unclear if CPO’s gains persist or diminish. The cost-effectiveness of CPO’s data generation (e.g., ~6.5k pairs for 200 samples, per Appendix A) for billion-scale models also needs evaluation.  
5. **Interpretability Gap:** While CPO improves accuracy, it lacks analysis of *why* optimized paths are superior. For instance, do preferred thoughts exhibit higher logical coherence or error robustness? Case studies (Table 6) are illustrative but superficial; a quantitative framework (e.g., fidelity scores for reasoning steps) would deepen insight.  

**Limitations:**  
The paper acknowledges computational costs of ToT data generation (Section 5.1) and the narrow scope of tasks (Section 7). However, two limitations deserve emphasis:  
- **Data Efficiency:** Figure 3c reveals CPO requires >120 instances to surpass base performance, suggesting sensitivity to training scale. This may hinder deployment in low-data domains.  
- **Ethical Risks:** Section "Societal Impacts" notes potential misuse but downplays CPO-specific risks (e.g., fine-tuning on biased ToT outputs could amplify reasoning inaccuracies in sensitive applications like fact verification).  

**Recommendation:**  
This work makes a valuable contribution by repurposing ToT’s search dynamics for efficient CoT improvement. The strengths in methodology and evaluation outweigh weaknesses, though the latter highlight avenues for future work (e.g., MATH benchmark, diversity metrics). The paper meets NeurIPS standards but falls short of transformative impact due to limited scope and unaddressed scalability.  

**Rating:**  
6 (marginally above threshold)  

**Justification for Rating:**  
The rating reflects CPO’s novel integration of preference learning with complex reasoning, strong empirical results, and clarity. Weaknesses are substantive but not fatal; they represent typical gaps in an otherwise well-executed study. The preserved decision aligns with the paper’s solid but incremental advance over existing paradigms (e.g., TS-SFT, FPO).

reviews.summary: The paper presents a novel method to enhance the performance of LLMs in complex problem-solving tasks using Chain of Preference Optimization (CPO). The authors propose fine-tuning LLMs using the search tree constructed by tree-of-thought (ToT), allowing CoT to achieve similar or better results without the heavy tree-searching during inference. The CPO method aligns each step of the CoT reasoning paths with those of ToT, leveraging the inherent preference information in the tree-search process. The authors provide experimental evidence of the effectiveness of CPO, showing significant improvements in LLM performance in tasks such as question answering, fact verification, and arithmetic reasoning.
reviews.strengths: The paper presents a novel method, Chain of Preference Optimization (CPO), that significantly enhances the CoT reasoning ability of LLMs without increasing the inference load. It offers a convincing comparison with the ToT method, demonstrating that CPO substantially reduces inference time, as evidenced by the decrease in latency from 1749s/ins to 38s/ins on Llama2-7B. Furthermore, the paper shows that CPO not only matches but surpasses the ToT baseline in terms of accuracy across multiple datasets. This indicates that CPO effectively fine-tunes LLMs to generate more optimal reasoning paths, thereby improving their performance in complex problem-solving tasks. The research is significant as it provides a practical solution to the challenge of balancing inference complexity and reasoning quality in LLMs.
reviews.weaknesses: The paper presents a promising approach to fine-tuning LLMs using CPO. However, there is a significant gap in the evaluation of the potential impacts of this fine-tuning process on other abilities of the pretrained LLMs. This omission could leave readers questioning the broader implications and versatility of the proposed method. Future work should consider a comprehensive evaluation of the fine-tuning process to fully understand its effects on the LLMs' overall performance.

Summary:
The paper presents a novel method to enhance the performance of LLMs in complex problem-solving tasks using Chain of Preference Optimization (CPO). The authors propose fine-tuning LLMs using the search tree constructed by tree-of-thought (ToT), allowing CoT to achieve similar or better results without the heavy tree-searching during inference. The CPO method aligns each step of the CoT reasoning paths with those of ToT, leveraging the inherent preference information in the tree-search process. The authors provide experimental evidence of the effectiveness of CPO, showing significant improvements in LLM performance in tasks such as question answering, fact verification, and arithmetic reasoning.

Strengths:
The paper presents a novel method, Chain of Preference Optimization (CPO), that significantly enhances the CoT reasoning ability of LLMs without increasing the inference load. It offers a convincing comparison with the ToT method, demonstrating that CPO substantially reduces inference time, as evidenced by the decrease in latency from 1749s/ins to 38s/ins on Llama2-7B. Furthermore, the paper shows that CPO not only matches but surpasses the ToT baseline in terms of accuracy across multiple datasets. This indicates that CPO effectively fine-tunes LLMs to generate more optimal reasoning paths, thereby improving their performance in complex problem-solving tasks. The research is significant as it provides a practical solution to the challenge of balancing inference complexity and reasoning quality in LLMs.

Weaknesses:
The paper presents a promising approach to fine-tuning LLMs using CPO. However, there is a significant gap in the evaluation of the potential impacts of this fine-tuning process on other abilities of the pretrained LLMs. This omission could leave readers questioning the broader implications and versatility of the proposed method. Future work should consider a comprehensive evaluation of the fine-tuning process to fully understand its effects on the LLMs' overall performance.
