title: Deep Support Vectors

reviews.summary: This paper introduces a novel Deep Support Vectors (DSVs) framework, which can be used to reconstruct data and serve as latent generative models using logits as latent. By adapting the traditional Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) condition for deep learning models, the authors introduce the DeepKKT one and show that generated DSVs using this condition exhibit properties similar to traditional support vectors but apply to modern deep architectures like ConvNet and ResNet. Experiments on several common image datasets, including CIFAR10, CIFAR100, SVHN, and ImageNet, verify that DeepKKT can extract and generate DSVs. Moreover, DSVs are shown to be better than existing algorithms in few-shot dataset distillation problems.
reviews.strengths: The paper is well-written and easy to follow. Introducing deep support vectors based on an adaption of the KKT condition used in traditional support vector machines (SVM) is novel and interesting. To be honest, I did not have enough time to verify all of the mathematical verifications in the paper, and I'll leave it in other reviewers' comments on this part.  The experimental results consistently show the benefit of DVSs in several problems, such as few-shot dataset distillation and image generation.
reviews.weaknesses:
One of the proposed DSVs' most critical limitations is requiring the model to be fully pre-trained before being applied for support vector generation. This condition differs from the traditional support vector machine (SVM) framework, where the training process of the (linear) model and the support vector selection are jointly performed. Also, it is unclear how the deep model is pre-trained (e.g., with which datasets; are there any domain gap issues between the pre-training data and the main training data, etc.). Furthermore, the margin definition in Eq. (3) is unclear, and I could not understand why the larger margin is better. 

Regarding the total training loss for the DSVs generation in (9), it is unclear about the selections of the augmentation operator $\mathcal A$. Moreover, when training (9) with the traditional SGD approach, how do we guarantee that the generated deep support vectors belong to the original training data set?
reviews.questions: Please address my questions in the weaknesses section.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper introduces a novel Deep Support Vectors (DSVs) framework, which can be used to reconstruct data and serve as latent generative models using logits as latent. By adapting the traditional Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) condition for deep learning models, the authors introduce the DeepKKT one and show that generated DSVs using this condition exhibit properties similar to traditional support vectors but apply to modern deep architectures like ConvNet and ResNet. Experiments on several common image datasets, including CIFAR10, CIFAR100, SVHN, and ImageNet, verify that DeepKKT can extract and generate DSVs. Moreover, DSVs are shown to be better than existing algorithms in few-shot dataset distillation problems.

Strengths:
The paper is well-written and easy to follow. Introducing deep support vectors based on an adaption of the KKT condition used in traditional support vector machines (SVM) is novel and interesting. To be honest, I did not have enough time to verify all of the mathematical verifications in the paper, and I'll leave it in other reviewers' comments on this part.  The experimental results consistently show the benefit of DVSs in several problems, such as few-shot dataset distillation and image generation.

Weaknesses:
One of the proposed DSVs' most critical limitations is requiring the model to be fully pre-trained before being applied for support vector generation. This condition differs from the traditional support vector machine (SVM) framework, where the training process of the (linear) model and the support vector selection are jointly performed. Also, it is unclear how the deep model is pre-trained (e.g., with which datasets; are there any domain gap issues between the pre-training data and the main training data, etc.). Furthermore, the margin definition in Eq. (3) is unclear, and I could not understand why the larger margin is better. 

Regarding the total training loss for the DSVs generation in (9), it is unclear about the selections of the augmentation operator $\mathcal A$. Moreover, when training (9) with the traditional SGD approach, how do we guarantee that the generated deep support vectors belong to the original training data set?

Questions:
Please address my questions in the weaknesses section.

Rating:
6
reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

**Summary:**  
This paper presents a novel framework for Deep Support Vectors (DSVs), which adapts the traditional Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) conditions to deep learning via the proposed *DeepKKT* condition. The authors demonstrate that DSVs enable data reconstruction, serve as latent generative models using logits as latent variables (Sec. 5.2), and exhibit properties analogous to classical SVM support vectors. Experiments validate DSVs on image datasets (ImageNet, CIFAR10/100, SVHN) using architectures like ResNet and ConvNet, showing advantages in few-shot dataset distillation (Table 2) and model inversion/generation (Fig. 1, 4, 6). The work bridges theoretical concepts from SVMs with modern deep learning, offering interpretability and generative capabilities.

**Strengths:**  
The paper is well-structured and clearly articulates its contributions. The core idea of extending KKT conditions to deep learning to derive DSVs is **novel and theoretically grounded**, providing a fresh perspective on model interpretability and generative modeling. The experimental validation is **comprehensive**:  
- DSVs outperform baselines (DC, DSA, DM) in few-shot distillation (Table 2), especially in low-data regimes (e.g., 21.68% accuracy with *zero* real images on CIFAR10).  
- The generative applications (Sec. 5.2) are compelling, particularly the use of soft labels as latent variables for image editing (Fig. 6) and transforming classifiers into generative models (Fig. 1).  
- The analysis of DSV properties (e.g., entropy near decision boundaries in Fig. 2a, λ-correlation with classwise accuracy in Fig. 2b) robustly supports their "support vector-like" behavior.  

**Weaknesses:**  
1. **Pre-training Dependency**: DSV generation *requires* a fully pre-trained model (Sec. 4.2), unlike traditional SVMs where support vectors emerge *during* training. This limits applicability to new tasks without costly pre-training. Crucially, the paper does not address **domain gaps** between pre-training data (e.g., ImageNet) and target tasks—a significant concern for real-world use.  
2. **Ambiguous Margin Definition**: The primal feasibility condition (Eq. 3) enforces a margin *ϵ* but lacks justification for why a *larger* margin is desirable or how *ϵ* is chosen. This is a notable omission given the centrality of margins to SVM theory.  
3. **Manifold Alignment via Augmentation**: The augmentation strategy $\mathcal{A}$ in Eq. 9 is critical for ensuring DSVs lie on the data manifold, but its design (e.g., translation/crop parameters in App. D) appears ad hoc. The paper does not justify why the chosen augmentations suffice for manifold alignment or how sensitive results are to these choices.  
4. **Proximity to Training Data**: While synthesized DSVs resemble training data (Fig. 7), the mechanism guaranteeing their fidelity to the *original training set* (rather than a generalized manifold) is unclear. This raises concerns about privacy (Sec. A) and whether DSVs truly "reconstruct" the model’s training data.  

**Questions:**  
- How does the choice of pre-training data impact DSV quality for downstream tasks (e.g., pre-training on ImageNet vs. CIFAR for SVHN distillation)?  
- Why is a larger margin in Eq. (3) beneficial? Does varying *ϵ* affect DSV properties?  
- Can the augmentation set $\mathcal{A}$ be optimized or ablated to validate its necessity?  

**Decision**:  
This paper introduces a conceptually innovative framework with strong empirical results. The limitations (pre-training dependency, margin ambiguity) are offset by its novel contributions to interpretability and generative modeling. The work merits acceptance but would benefit from addressing the weaknesses above.  

**Rating**:  
6 (Borderline Accept)

Strengths:
The paper is well-written and easy to follow. Introducing deep support vectors based on an adaption of the KKT condition used in traditional support vector machines (SVM) is novel and interesting. To be honest, I did not have enough time to verify all of the mathematical verifications in the paper, and I'll leave it in other reviewers' comments on this part.  The experimental results consistently show the benefit of DVSs in several problems, such as few-shot dataset distillation and image generation.

Weaknesses:
One of the proposed DSVs' most critical limitations is requiring the model to be fully pre-trained before being applied for support vector generation. This condition differs from the traditional support vector machine (SVM) framework, where the training process of the (linear) model and the support vector selection are jointly performed. Also, it is unclear how the deep model is pre-trained (e.g., with which datasets; are there any domain gap issues between the pre-training data and the main training data, etc.). Furthermore, the margin definition in Eq. (3) is unclear, and I could not understand why the larger margin is better. 

Regarding the total training loss for the DSVs generation in (9), it is unclear about the selections of the augmentation operator $\mathcal A$. Moreover, when training (9) with the traditional SGD approach, how do we guarantee that the generated deep support vectors belong to the original training data set?

Questions:
Please address my questions in the weaknesses section.

Rating:
6

reviews.summary:
The paper describes rethinking a classification through support vector in a way similar to SVMs. It provides additional benefits such as: 
- few-shot dataset distillation
- using the similarity of the proposed formulation to the diffusion models, the model can be transformed into a generative model
reviews.strengths:
- Originality: The paper demonstrates out-of-the-box thinking by showing that generalisation of the deep KKT conditions leads to similarity with diffusion models; the contribution of the paper is multifaceted: formulating deep KKT conditions, showing the utility of such formulation for dataset distillation, and showing how it could lead to a generative model. 
- Significance: I think this work is significant as the authors contribute towards the intersection of such important problems as dataset distillation, transparency of the ML models, and provide new insight about the connection between the KKT conditions and the diffusion models
- Clarity: the paper is clearly written, the only optional suggestion, for the ease of mathematical notation, is to use the boldface fonts for vectors, e.g., like Bishop (2006), page xi.  It also gives, in my understanding, enough reproducibility information. 
- Quality: The paper presents and sufficiently backs up the claims.
reviews.weaknesses: Clarity: the authors are incouraged to improve the discussion over the limitations.
reviews.questions:
1) It would be useful if the authors have any comments on the failure modes of the proposed model. For example, one of the aspects may be the confounding factors (Bontempelli et al (2022)) which could be picked up by the model? 

2) One of the perceived limitations is that the support vectors only represent the whole image. It does not allow to pick up the particular aspects of the image which takes the decision. For example, we can guess from an image of a cat with antlers that these are the antlers which make it look like a deer. But strictly speaking we don't know if such changes result in confirmation bias or whether the model actually predicts the results based on the antlers. I wonder if the authors can comment on such limitation?

3) I could imagine such work is also linked with incremental/lifelong learning, for example in a way similar to Laskov et al (2006), which considers incremental SVM. In contrast to the standard deep-learning solutions, KKT can be updated recursively, which provides a wealth of options for improving the performance. This might be an additional benefit of the proposed formulation. I wonder if the authors can comment on this? 

Bontempelli et al (2022) Concept-level Debugging of Part-Prototype Networks, ICLR 2022

Laskov, P., Gehl, C., Krüger, S., Müller, K.R., Bennett, K.P. and Parrado-Hernández, E., 2006. Incremental support vector learning: Analysis, implementation and applications. Journal of machine learning research, 7(9).
reviews.limitations: As discussed above, the section on limitations would greatly improve the discussion.

reviews.text:
### Expanded Review  

#### **Summary**  
The paper introduces a novel framework for identifying Deep Support Vectors (DSVs) in deep learning models by generalizing the Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) conditions to the *DeepKKT* formulation. This approach bridges the interpretability of SVMs with the flexibility of deep networks, enabling:  
- **Few-shot dataset distillation** by reconstructing models from minimal DSVs.  
- **Model inversion** to generate high-fidelity images from pretrained classifiers.  
- **Latent generative modeling** by leveraging logits as latent variables.  
The authors validate DSVs on ImageNet, CIFAR10/100, and SVHN using ResNet/ConvNet architectures, demonstrating competitive performance in distillation tasks and novel applications like decision-boundary visualization (Fig 1, 3, 6).  

---

#### **Strengths**  
1. **Originality & Multifaceted Contributions**:  
   - The *DeepKKT* condition is a theoretically grounded extension of SVM principles to deep learning (Sec 4.1). It elegantly adapts stationarity and feasibility conditions to high-dimensional, multi-class settings, formalizing the link between classifier gradients and support vectors.  
   - The work unifies disparate areas—dataset distillation, model interpretability, and generative modeling—under a single framework. For instance, Sec 5.2 shows classifiers can generate images via latent interpolation (Fig 6, 11), revealing an unexpected connection to diffusion models (Eq 10–11).  

2. **Significance**:  
   - DSVs address critical limitations of deep learning: massive data requirements and black-box decisions. By distilling models into compact DSV sets (e.g., 1–10 images per class in Table 2), the method enables efficient model deployment in resource-constrained settings (e.g., federated learning).  
   - The generative application (Sec 5.2) transforms classifiers into data-efficient latent models, bypassing dedicated GAN/diffusion training. The ImageNet results (Fig 1, 13) showcase high fidelity, suggesting broader utility beyond classification.  

3. **Clarity & Reproducibility**:  
   - The paper is well-structured, with clear algorithmic descriptions (Algorithm 1) and ablation studies (Fig 5). Experimental details (datasets, hyperparameters, architectures) are thoroughly documented in Sec D (Appendix), facilitating replication.  

4. **Technical Quality**:  
   - Claims are robustly supported: DSVs outperform baselines in distillation (Table 2), encode decision boundaries (Fig 3), and exhibit SVM-like properties (Fig 2a–b). The manifold regularization (Eq 9) effectively constrains DSVs to realistic images (Fig 7).  

---

#### **Weaknesses**  
1. **Limited Discussion of Failure Modes**:  
   - While Sec B notes the *intuitive* (vs. rigorous) equivalence between DSVs and SVM support vectors, it omits practical failure cases. For example:  
     - How do confounding factors (e.g., spurious correlations like "deer with antlers") impact DSV reliability? The deer/antler example (Sec 1) highlights bias but does not quantify mitigation strategies.  
     - In generative tasks, Fig 5 shows primal-loss-only optimization yields low-fidelity images, but the paper lacks analysis of edge cases (e.g., class entanglement in latent interpolation).  

2. **Scope of Interpretability**:  
   - DSVs provide global explanations (e.g., class-wise decision criteria in Fig 3) but cannot localize *pixel-level* evidence (unlike saliency maps). For instance, while editing an image to add "facial dots" converts a cat to a dog (Fig 3), this may not reveal *why* those dots are pivotal. Incorporating local explicability (e.g., gradient-based attribution) could strengthen this.  

3. **Broader Limitations (Expanded from Sec B)**:  
   - **Computational Cost**: DSV synthesis requires multiple forward/backward passes per sample (Sec 4.2). While lighter than diffusion models, scalability to billion-parameter models (e.g., ViT-22B) is unverified.  
   - **Theoretical Gaps**: The stationarity condition (Eq 5) assumes overparameterized models converge to SVM-like solutions (Sec C), but real-world deviations (e.g., non-convex losses) are not analyzed.  

---

#### **Questions & Suggestions**  
1. **Failure Modes & Robustness**:  
   - Have you evaluated how dataset bias (e.g., background features in CIFAR-10) affects DSV semantics? Quantifying this via perturbation analysis (e.g., masking "antlers" in deer DSVs) could clarify robustness.  

2. **Local vs. Global Explanations**:  
   - Can DSVs be integrated with local explanation methods (e.g., Grad-CAM) to highlight *specific regions* within support vectors? This might address the "whole-image limitation" noted in the review.  

3. **Incremental Learning Connections**:  
   - The KKT stationarity condition (Eq 5) resembles recursive SVM updates (e.g., Laskov et al., 2006). Could DSVs enable efficient continual learning? For example, fine-tuning DSVs instead of full datasets for new classes would align with the distillation strengths shown in Table 2.  

4. **Societal Impact Expansion**:  
   - While Sec A notes DSVs enhance responsible AI, privacy risks from model inversion (Fig 4) warrant deeper discussion. For instance, could generated DSVs leak sensitive training data? Fig 7 suggests they generalize boundaries, but adversarial edge cases should be explored.  

---

#### **Conclusion**  
This work makes significant contributions by redefining support vectors for deep learning. The *DeepKKT* framework is novel, well-validated, and impactful across distillation, interpretability, and generation. Though limitations in theoretical rigor and local explicability remain, the paper’s strengths far outweigh these concerns. I recommend **acceptance** with a rating of **8**, encouraging the authors to address the raised questions in future work.  

**Rating: 8** (Accept)

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper introduces Deep Support Vectors (DSVs), an adaptation of support vector concepts to deep learning models. The authors propose a DeepKKT condition, analogous to the KKT conditions in SVMs, to identify or generate DSVs in trained deep models. They demonstrate that DSVs exhibit properties similar to traditional support vectors, including encoding decision boundaries and enabling model reconstruction. The paper shows applications of DSVs in few-shot dataset distillation, model interpretability, and even using classification models as latent generative models. The authors validate their approach on common datasets (ImageNet, CIFAR10, CIFAR100) and architectures (ResNet, ConvNet).

Strengths:
- Novel concept of Deep Support Vectors that bridges ideas from SVMs to deep learning
- Evaluation across multiple datasets and model architectures
- Diverse applications including dataset distillation, model interpretability, and generative modeling
- Provides both theoretical motivation and empirical validation

Weaknesses:
- Transformer architectures have not been included in the experiments
- Comparison to other interpretability methods is limited such as global SHAP (mean absolute SHAP value)  or other global methods 
- The proposed method has limitation when it comes to real-scenario applications. Dedicated generative models such as GANs or Transformers are better options.

Questions:
- How sensitive are the DSVs to the choice of hyperparameters in the DeepKKT condition? Is there a principled way to select these parameters?
- How well do DSVs scale to larger models and datasets? Are there computational challenges in applying this approach to state-of-the-art large language models, for instance?

Limitations:
A more in-depth consideration of privacy implications on the generative capability of the method could be given

Rating:
6
reviews.text:
### Expanded Peer Review

**Summary:**  
This paper introduces Deep Support Vectors (DSVs), an innovative adaptation of support vector concepts to deep learning models. The authors propose the *DeepKKT condition*, a generalization of the Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) conditions from SVMs, to identify or generate DSVs in trained deep networks. These DSVs exhibit properties akin to traditional support vectors, such as encoding decision boundaries and enabling model reconstruction from minimal data. The paper demonstrates DSVs' utility in few-shot dataset distillation, model interpretability, and repurposing classifiers as latent generative models. Experiments validate the approach on ImageNet, CIFAR10, and CIFAR100 using ResNet and ConvNet architectures, with compelling visualizations (e.g., Figure 1 generated images, Figure 3 decision-boundary edits). The work bridges theoretical SVM principles with practical deep learning challenges, though certain architectural and scalability gaps remain.

---

### **Strengths**  
1. **Novel Conceptual Bridge**: The DeepKKT condition elegantly translates SVM theory (e.g., support vectors, stationarity) to deep learning, addressing data inefficiency and black-box interpretability. The formalization (Sec. 4.1) and derivation of Eq. 5–6 provide a principled foundation for DSVs.  
2. **Rigorous Multi-Dataset/Architecture Validation**: Experiments span diverse settings:  
   - *Dataset distillation* (Table 2): DSVs outperform DSA, DM, and DC in low-data regimes (e.g., 0-shot CIFAR10: 21.68% vs. ≤16.48%).  
   - *Model inversion* (Fig. 4): DSVs reconstruct high-fidelity images from fine-tuned models (e.g., SVHN adapted from CIFAR10 weights).  
   - *Generative repurposing* (Fig. 6): Latent interpolation (e.g., "lobster" + "harp" fusion) confirms classifiers can generate novel samples via soft labels.  
3. **Diverse Applications**: DSVs enable:  
   - *Interpretability*: Global decision criteria (e.g., antlers for "deer" in Fig. 3) via visual DSV properties.  
   - *Efficient distillation*: Hessian-free, training-data-agnostic synthesis (Sec. 5.1).  
   - *Generative modeling*: Classifiers as latent generators (Sec. 5.2), leveraging logits as latents.  
4. **Theoretical-Empirical Synergy**: The DeepKKT derivation (Sec. 4.1) is grounded in SVM theory, while experiments (e.g., entropy analysis in Fig. 2a) empirically validate DSV behavior. Appendix C offers intuitive links to overparameterized optimization dynamics.  

---

### **Weaknesses**  
1. **Limited Architecture Scope**: Experiments exclude *transformer-based models* (e.g., ViTs), which dominate modern vision tasks. The claim of "general architectures" (Abstract) is thus incompletely substantiated.  
2. **Incomplete Interpretability Comparisons**: While DSVs provide global explanations (Fig. 3), quantitative benchmarking against methods like *global SHAP* or *concept activation vectors* is absent. For instance, no metrics (e.g., fidelity, robustness) compare DSV-based explanations to existing XAI tools.  
3. **Practical Generative Limitations**: Despite promising results (Fig. 1, 6), DSV-based generation:  
   - *Lacks efficiency metrics* (e.g., FID scores) versus GANs/diffusion models.  
   - *Struggles with structural coherence* in complex edits (Fig. 14: "yacht" → "scarf" distorts geometry). Dedicated generative models (e.g., SDXL) remain superior for real-world synthesis.  
4. **Privacy Implications Underexplored**: Sec. A mentions DSVs avoid replicating training samples (Fig. 7), but risks of *model inversion attacks* (e.g., extracting sensitive attributes via Fig. 4) need deeper analysis.  

---

### **Questions**  
1. **Hyperparameter Sensitivity**: The loss (Eq. 9) uses \( \beta_1, \beta_2, \beta_3 \) for manifold regularization. How do DSVs vary with these? Is there a principled way to set them (e.g., task-specific validation)?  
2. **Scalability**: Can DeepKKT scale to *billion-parameter models* (e.g., LLMs) or web-scale datasets? The dual-gradient computation (Alg. 1) may face memory bottlenecks.  
3. **Real-World Distillation Feasibility**: For federated learning (Sec. 5.1), how does communication overhead of transmitting DSVs compare to gradient-based schemes?  

---

### **Limitations**  
- **Privacy Risks**: While societal impacts (Appx. A) note DSVs avoid replicating training data, the generative capability (Sec. 5.2) could *invert sensitive models* (e.g., medical classifiers). Mitigation strategies (e.g., differential privacy) are unaddressed.  
- **Theoretical Gaps**: The DeepKKT equivalence to SVM support vectors (Sec. 4.1) is intuitive but lacks *formal convergence guarantees* (noted in Sec. B).  
- **Architectural Generalizability**: Claims of "general applicability" are weakened by omitting non-CNN architectures (e.g., transformers, MLPs).  

---

### **Recommendations for Improvement**  
- Include **transformer experiments** (e.g., ViT on ImageNet) to strengthen generality claims.  
- Add **quantitative XAI comparisons** (e.g., against Integrated Gradients or SHAP) to contextualize interpretability gains.  
- Discuss **privacy safeguards** (e.g., noise injection during DSV generation) in response to societal impacts.  

---

### **Rating**  
**6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold**  
The work introduces a theoretically grounded and empirically validated method with compelling applications. While limitations exist (e.g., architectural coverage, privacy depth), the novel fusion of SVM principles and deep learning offers significant value for model efficiency and interpretability.

Summary:
This paper introduces Deep Support Vectors (DSVs), an adaptation of support vector concepts to deep learning models. The authors propose a DeepKKT condition, analogous to the KKT conditions in SVMs, to identify or generate DSVs in trained deep models. They demonstrate that DSVs exhibit properties similar to traditional support vectors, including encoding decision boundaries and enabling model reconstruction. The paper shows applications of DSVs in few-shot dataset distillation, model interpretability, and even using classification models as latent generative models. The authors validate their approach on common datasets (ImageNet, CIFAR10, CIFAR100) and architectures (ResNet, ConvNet).

Strengths:
- Novel concept of Deep Support Vectors that bridges ideas from SVMs to deep learning
- Evaluation across multiple datasets and model architectures
- Diverse applications including dataset distillation, model interpretability, and generative modeling
- Provides both theoretical motivation and empirical validation

Weaknesses:
- Transformer architectures have not been included in the experiments
- Comparison to other interpretability methods is limited such as global SHAP (mean absolute SHAP value)  or other global methods 
- The proposed method has limitation when it comes to real-scenario applications. Dedicated generative models such as GANs or Transformers are better options.

Questions:
- How sensitive are the DSVs to the choice of hyperparameters in the DeepKKT condition? Is there a principled way to select these parameters?

6

reviews.summary: This paper introduces the concept of Deep Support Vectors (DSVs) as an adaptation of support vectors from Support Vector Machines (SVMs) to deep learning models. The authors propose the DeepKKT condition, which generalizes the traditional Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) conditions of SVMs to handle the high-dimensional and multi-class nature of deep learning problems. By selecting or generating DSVs that satisfy the DeepKKT condition, the authors demonstrate that these vectors can play a similar role to traditional support vectors in terms of encoding decision boundaries and reconstructing models from a small subset of samples. The paper also shows that DSVs can be used for few-shot dataset distillation and as a means to alleviate the black-box characteristics of deep learning models by providing visual explanations of decision criteria. Furthermore, the authors demonstrate that the DeepKKT condition can transform conventional classification models into generative models with high fidelity, using class labels as latent variables.
reviews.strengths:
1. The paper introduces a novel concept, Deep Support Vectors, which extends the idea of support vectors from SVMs to deep learning models, providing a new perspective on understanding deep neural networks.

2. The proposed DeepKKT condition effectively generalizes the traditional KKT conditions to handle the complexities of deep learning problems, such as high dimensionality and multi-class classification.

3. The authors demonstrate the practical applicability of DSVs through various experiments, including few-shot dataset distillation, visual explanation of decision criteria, and transforming classification models into generative models.

4. The paper provides a thorough comparison of DSVs with existing methods in the context of few-shot dataset distillation, highlighting the superiority of DSVs under practical constraints.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. The paper lacks a rigorous mathematical derivation of the DeepKKT condition and its connection to traditional KKT conditions. A more formal treatment would strengthen the theoretical foundation of the proposed method.

2. While the authors provide experimental evidence for the effectiveness of DSVs, a more comprehensive analysis of the method's sensitivity to hyperparameters and its robustness to different architectures and datasets would be beneficial.

3. The paper does not provide a detailed discussion on the computational complexity of generating DSVs and how it scales with the size of the dataset and the complexity of the model.
reviews.questions: See Weaknesses.
reviews.limitations: See Weaknesses.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper introduces the concept of Deep Support Vectors (DSVs) as an adaptation of support vectors from Support Vector Machines (SVMs) to deep learning models. The authors propose the DeepKKT condition, which generalizes the traditional Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) conditions of SVMs to handle the high-dimensional and multi-class nature of deep learning problems. By selecting or generating DSVs that satisfy the DeepKKT condition, the authors demonstrate that these vectors can play a similar role to traditional support vectors in terms of encoding decision boundaries and reconstructing models from a small subset of samples. The paper also shows that DSVs can be used for few-shot dataset distillation and as a means to alleviate the black-box characteristics of deep learning models by providing visual explanations of decision criteria. Furthermore, the authors demonstrate that the DeepKKT condition can transform conventional classification models into generative models with high fidelity, using class labels as latent variables.

Strengths:
1. The paper introduces a novel concept, Deep Support Vectors, which extends the idea of support vectors from SVMs to deep learning models, providing a new perspective on understanding deep neural networks.

2. The proposed DeepKKT condition effectively generalizes the traditional KKT conditions to handle the complexities of deep learning problems, such as high dimensionality and multi-class classification.

3. The authors demonstrate the practical applicability of DSVs through various experiments, including few-shot dataset distillation, visual explanation of decision criteria, and transforming classification models into generative models.

4. The paper provides a thorough comparison of DSVs with existing methods in the context of few-shot dataset distillation, highlighting the superiority of DSVs under practical constraints.

Weaknesses:
1. The paper lacks a rigorous mathematical derivation of the DeepKKT condition and its connection to traditional KKT conditions. A more formal treatment would strengthen the theoretical foundation of the proposed method.

2. While the authors provide experimental evidence for the effectiveness of DSVs, a more comprehensive analysis of the method's sensitivity to hyperparameters and its robustness to different architectures and datasets would be beneficial.

3. The paper does not provide a detailed discussion on the computational complexity of generating DSVs and how it scales with the size of the dataset and the complexity of the model.

Questions:
See Weaknesses.

Limitations:
See Weaknesses.

Rating:
5
reviews.text:
**Expanded Review**

**Summary:**  
This paper introduces Deep Support Vectors (DSVs), a novel adaptation of SVM support vectors to deep learning, proposing the DeepKKT condition to generalize traditional KKT conditions for high-dimensional, multi-class settings. The authors validate DSVs through experiments on few-shot distillation, decision boundary visualization, and generative modeling. While the work is theoretically ambitious and empirically thorough, certain limitations in mathematical rigor and practical scalability warrant attention.

**Strengths:**  
1. **Conceptual Novelty:** The paper makes a significant conceptual leap by bridging SVMs and deep learning through DSVs. The adaptation of KKT conditions to the DeepKKT framework (Section 4.1) is particularly innovative, addressing nonlinearity and multi-class challenges inherent to deep models. The entropy analysis in Figure 2a, showing DSV candidates approaching decision boundaries, provides empirical grounding for this connection.  

2. **Practical Validation:** The experiments are comprehensive and well-structured. For instance, Table 2 demonstrates DSVs outperforming DSA and DM in 10-shot CIFAR10 distillation (37.9% vs. 36.63%), validating their utility in data-efficient scenarios. The latent interpolation results (Figure 6) and ImageNet inversions (Figure 1) showcase DSVs’ generative capabilities, extending beyond mere theoretical interest.  

3. **Interpretability Contributions:** The visual explanations in Figures 3 and 7 highlight DSVs’ role in demystifying decision criteria (e.g., antlers for deer classification). This aligns with the paper’s goal of mitigating black-box limitations, offering a tangible advance over saliency maps or feature attribution methods.  

4. **Architectural Flexibility:** Experiments on ResNet50 (ImageNet) and ConvNet (CIFAR/SVHN) confirm DSVs’ applicability to standard architectures. The transfer learning results in Figure 4, where only the last layer is fine-tuned, further underscore the method’s adaptability.  

**Weaknesses:**  
1. **Theoretical Gaps:** While the DeepKKT condition is motivated intuitively (Section 4.1), the paper lacks a formal derivation linking it to the original KKT framework. For example, the stationarity condition (Equation 5) is presented as an analogy rather than a rigorous extension, and the omission of complementary slackness (Section 4.2) weakens the theoretical equivalence claim. Appendix C’s dynamical systems analogy partially addresses this but does not substitute for a mathematical proof.  

2. **Hyperparameter Sensitivity:** The method relies heavily on hyperparameters like β1-β3 in Equation 9 and augmentation strengths (Appendix D). However, the paper provides no ablation study or sensitivity analysis. For instance, the impact of β1 (primal loss weight) on DSV fidelity in Figure 5 is noted anecdotally but not quantified, leaving practitioners without guidance for tuning.  

3. **Computational Scalability:** Although Algorithm 1 claims efficiency via “two backward passes,” the actual compute costs are unclear. Training DSVs for ImageNet (Section 5.2) likely requires significant resources, given the need for gradient updates across high-resolution images. The paper does not compare wall-clock times against baselines like dataset distillation (Table 2) or diffusion models (Section 5.2), limiting practical assessment.  

4. **Limited Robustness Analysis:** The experiments focus on controlled settings (e.g., pretrained models on standard datasets). There is no exploration of DSV stability under distribution shifts or adversarial perturbations, despite the deer/antler example in the introduction. Figure 10’s adversarial mixing experiment hints at this but lacks systematic evaluation.  

**Questions:**  
1. Can the authors provide a bound on the approximation error introduced by neglecting complementary slackness in DeepKKT?  
2. How does DSV generation scale with model depth (e.g., ResNet101 vs. ResNet50) or input dimension (e.g., higher-resolution images)?  

**Limitations:**  
The work assumes access to a fully trained model, limiting applicability to scenarios where model parameters are opaque (e.g., cloud APIs). Additionally, the manifold condition (Section 4.2) relies on handcrafted priors (total variation, norms), which may not generalize to non-visual domains like text or tabular data.  

**Recommendation:**  
The paper’s strengths in conceptual innovation and empirical validation outweigh its theoretical and practical limitations. The introduction of DSVs opens new avenues for interpretable deep learning, and the demonstrated applications in distillation and generation are compelling. However, addressing the identified gaps in future work—particularly rigorous theoretical grounding and scalability analysis—will be critical for broader adoption.  

**Rating:** 5 (Accept)
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper introduces the concept of Deep Support Vectors (DSVs) as an adaptation of support vectors from Support Vector Machines (SVMs) to deep learning models. The authors propose the DeepKKT condition, which generalizes the traditional Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) conditions of SVMs to handle the high-dimensional and multi-class nature of deep learning problems. By selecting or generating DSVs that satisfy the DeepKKT condition, the authors demonstrate that these vectors can play a similar role to traditional support vectors in terms of encoding decision boundaries and reconstructing models from a small subset of samples. The paper also shows that DSVs can be used for few-shot dataset distillation and as a means to alleviate the black-box characteristics of deep learning models by providing visual explanations of decision criteria. Furthermore, the authors demonstrate that the DeepKKT condition can transform conventional classification models into generative models with high fidelity, using class labels as latent variables.

Strengths:
1. The paper introduces a novel concept, Deep Support Vectors, which extends the idea of support vectors from SVMs to deep learning models, providing a new perspective on understanding deep neural networks.

2. The proposed DeepKKT condition effectively generalizes the traditional KKT conditions to handle the complexities of deep learning problems, such as high dimensionality and multi-class classification.

3. The authors demonstrate the practical applicability of DSVs through various experiments, including few-shot dataset distillation, visual explanation of decision criteria, and transforming classification models into generative models.

4. The paper provides a thorough comparison of DSVs with existing methods in the context of few-shot dataset distillation, highlighting the superiority of DSVs under practical constraints.

Weaknesses:
1. The paper lacks a rigorous mathematical derivation of the DeepKKT condition and its connection to traditional KKT conditions. A more formal treatment would strengthen the theoretical foundation of the proposed method.

2. While the authors provide experimental evidence for the effectiveness of DSVs, a more comprehensive analysis of the method's sensitivity to hyperparameters and its robustness to different architectures and datasets would be beneficial.

3. The paper does not provide a detailed discussion on the computational complexity of generating DSVs and how it scales with the size of the dataset and the complexity of the model.

Questions:
See Weaknesses.

Limitations:
See Weaknesses.

Rating:
5

reviews.summary:
This paper introduces the DeepKKT conditions for deep svm models, which correspond to the KKT condition in traditional linear SVMs. By either selecting deep support vectors (DSVs) from training data or generating them from already trained deep learning models, The authors show DSVs can play a similar role to conventional support vectors.

In addition,  this paper shows that the DeepKKT condition can transform  conventional classification models into generative models with high fidelity, particularly as latent generative models using class labels as latent variables. The experiments validate the effectiveness of DSVs using common datasets (ImageNet, CIFAR10 and CIFAR100) on the general architectures (ResNet and ConvNet).

Overall, the contribution is limited, hard to follow and I do not recommend for publication at this moment.
reviews.strengths: The DeepKKT and its extended version in Eq. 9 to generate DSVs is interesting. This paper shows that the DeepKKT condition can transform  conventional classification models into generative models with high fidelity, particularly as latent generative models using class labels as latent variables. The experiments validate the effectiveness of DSVs using common datasets (ImageNet, CIFAR10 and CIFAR100) on the general architectures (ResNet and ConvNet).
reviews.weaknesses:
(1) innovation and contribution
This paper is just deep svms, DeepKKT is known, and its contribution is trivial. Overall, the deep svms here is still a black-box. DeepKKT can relieve the black box issue by introducing deep learning to svms (line 34-36)? The heuristic method to generate DSVs using Eq.9 is not convincing. are they still DSVs?

(2) Technical and theoretical  analysis
In technical level, it may be practical to use Eq. 9 to generate DSVs (either from training data or generated), but it is heuristic.

(3) The paper idea is easy, but the writing is still needs to improve.
reviews.questions:
(1) For example, I do not follow "Like support vectors can reconstruct SVM, we can reconstruct the deep models from scratch only with DSVs.". How do you reconstruct deep svms from DSVs?

(2) deep learning is black box, and the authors using black box (deep learning here) to relieve this issue?
Is it possible to rewrite the paper to sell its idea on DeepKKT and how to generate DSVs?
reviews.limitations: see above.

In addition,  this paper shows that the DeepKKT condition can transform  conventional classification models into generative models with high fidelity, particularly as latent generative models using class labels as latent variables. The experiments validate the effectiveness of DSVs using common datasets (ImageNet, CIFAR10 and CIFAR100) on the general architectures (ResNet and ConvNet).

Overall, the contribution is limited, hard to follow and I do not recommend for publication at this moment.

Strengths:

This paper shows that the DeepKKT condition can transform  conventional classification models into generative models with high fidelity, particularly as latent generative models using class labels as latent variables. The experiments validate the effectiveness of DSVs using common datasets (ImageNet, CIFAR10 and CIFAR100) on the general architectures (ResNet and ConvNet).

Weaknesses:

Rating:
4
reviews.text:
### Expanded Review

**Summary:**  
This paper proposes the DeepKKT condition, an adaptation of the traditional KKT condition for deep learning models, to identify Deep Support Vectors (DSVs) that mimic the properties of support vectors in SVMs. The authors claim DSVs can address deep learning's limitations—specifically, its reliance on massive datasets and black-box decision-making—by enabling few-shot dataset distillation, model interpretability, and transformation of classifiers into generative models. Experiments on ImageNet, CIFAR10, and CIFAR100 with ResNet/ConvNet architectures validate DSVs' utility in distillation, model inversion, and latent-space image generation (Fig. 1, 4, 6). While the concept of leveraging KKT conditions for deep models is intriguing, the paper suffers from limited innovation, heuristic methodologies, and unclear exposition, which undermine its contributions.  

**Strengths:**  
1. **Generative Potential:** The application of DeepKKT to convert classifiers into latent generative models (Sec. 5.2) is a notable strength. By treating logits as latent variables (e.g., interpolating soft labels in ImageNet; Fig. 6), DSVs achieve high-fidelity image synthesis and editing (e.g., transforming a yacht’s sail into a scarf), demonstrating versatility beyond conventional classification.  
2. **Empirical Validation:** Experiments on large-scale datasets (e.g., ImageNet pretrained ResNet50) and architectures (Sec. 5) robustly showcase DSVs’ effectiveness in distillation (Table 2) and model inversion (Fig. 4). The comparison to trajectory-matching methods (DSA, DM) highlights DSVs’ superiority in low-data regimes.  
3. **Interpretability Insights:** DSVs provide intuitive visual explanations of decision boundaries (Fig. 3), such as antlers for deer classification, aligning with SVM-like "white-box" interpretability. This bridges deep learning’s opacity with classical SVM transparency.  

**Weaknesses:**  
1. **Limited Innovation:** The core concept—extending KKT conditions to deep models—lacks novelty. Prior work (e.g., Sec. 2.1–2.2) has explored similar connections (e.g., [6, 15, 28]), and the paper fails to rigorously differentiate DeepKKT beyond heuristic adaptations (e.g., Eq. 9). The claim that DSVs "alleviate black-box characteristics" (Abstract) is ironic since the method relies on deep networks themselves, perpetuating opacity.  
2. **Heuristic Methodology:** The DSV generation process (Eq. 9) is highly heuristic, combining augmentation, total variation, and norm penalties without theoretical justification. For instance:  
   - The manifold constraint (via augmentation $A(x)$) is pragmatic but lacks formal grounding in manifold learning theory.  
   - The exclusion of complementary slackness (Sec. 4.2) weakens the analogy to SVMs, relying instead on empirical entropy observations (Fig. 2a).  
3. **Unsubstantiated Claims:**  
   - **Model Reconstruction:** The assertion that DSVs can "reconstruct deep models from scratch" (Introduction) is unsupported. The paper only shows distillation via fine-tuning on DSVs (Table 2), not architectural or functional reconstruction.  
   - **Equivalence to SVMs:** The intuitive link between DSVs and SVM support vectors (Sec. 5.1) lacks formal proof. Appendix B acknowledges this, admitting equivalence is only "described intuitively."  
4. **Exposition Issues:**  
   - **Technical Ambiguity:** Algorithm 1 is poorly integrated with Eq. 9, omitting hyperparameter details (e.g., $\eta$, $\beta_i$ tuning). The stationarity loss $L_{\text{stat}}$ (Eq. 8) uses an $L_1$ distance without ablation.  
   - **Reproducibility Gaps:** While code is provided, key details (e.g., augmentation hyperparameters in Sec. D) are buried in appendices, hindering replication.  

**Questions:**  
1. **Model Reconstruction (Sec. 1):** How exactly do DSVs "reconstruct" deep models? The paper only demonstrates distillation (Table 2), not model rebuilding. If DSVs are merely data points for fine-tuning, this misrepresents SVM-style reconstruction.  
2. **Black-Box Paradox:** Can deep models truly "alleviate black-box characteristics" (Abstract) if DSVs depend on the same models? The approach seems self-referential—using a black box to explain itself—without resolving the core opacity issue.  

**Broader Limitations:**  
- **Theoretical Gaps:** The omission of complementary slackness (Sec. 4.2) and reliance on entropy (Fig. 2a) weaken the SVM analogy. A rigorous proof of DeepKKT’s convergence (Appendix C) is needed.  
- **Practical Scalability:** DSV generation requires multiple backpropagation steps per sample (Sec. 4.2), which may be prohibitive for billion-parameter models (e.g., SDXL mentioned in Sec. 1).  
- **Societal Impact:** While Sec. A highlights responsible AI, model inversion (Fig. 1) risks privacy breaches if applied to sensitive models (e.g., medical classifiers). Safeguards are unaddressed.  

**Recommendation:**  
The paper’s strengths—generative applications and empirical validation—are overshadowed by weak innovation, heuristic design, and unresolved contradictions (e.g., using deep learning to "solve" its own opacity). The writing exacerbates these issues via ambiguous claims and technical omissions. For these reasons, **I recommend rejection** (Rating 4).  

**Suggestions for Revision:**  
1. Formalize the connection between DSVs and SVM support vectors, including complementary slackness.  
2. Clarify "model reconstruction" with architectural/functional analyses.  
3. Ablate components of Eq. 9 to justify heuristic choices.  
4. Address the black-box paradox directly.  

---  
**Rating:** 4 (Reject)  
**Decision:** Unchanged from original review. Weaknesses in novelty, methodology, and clarity significantly diminish the paper’s contribution.

title: Abductive Reasoning in Logical Credal Networks

reviews.summary: This paper addresses abductive reasoning tasks such as generating MAP and Marginal MAP (MMAP) explanations in Logical Credal Networks (LCNs). Given that LCNs encode sets of distributions over their interpretations, a complete or partial explanation of the evidence may correspond to multiple distributions. Thus, the authors define the maximin/maximax MAP and MMAP tasks for LCNs as finding complete or partial MAP assignments with maximum lower/upper probability given the evidence. They propose several search algorithms that combine depth-first search, limited-discrepancy search, or simulated annealing with exact evaluations of MAP assignments using marginal inference for LCNs. Additionally, they develop an approximate message-passing scheme and extend limited discrepancy search and simulated annealing to use approximate evaluations of MAP assignments during the search. Experiments show that the approximation schemes which they have proposed can scale to much larger problems compared to search methods.
reviews.strengths:
1. The research is very detailed, providing an excellent formalization of abductive reasoning in LCNs. This formalization addresses a significant gap in previous LCN research, where solving MAP was challenging, and developing corresponding algorithms is not a trivial extension. The authors list several algorithms for solving this and successfully implement and compare them in detail.
2. The approximate algorithms proposed by the authors significantly improve both the solving time and the scale of solvable problems compared to previous search-based methods.
reviews.weaknesses: The experimental setup is relatively simple, lacking more practical industrial examples and relying more on basic toy experiments. Additionally, there is no comparison with other methods.
reviews.questions:
1. In the initial LCN papers, the authors compared LCN with ProbLog and MLN. Can your method be compared with these? For example, ProbLog is a direct derivative of logic programming, which can perform basic reasoning, including deductive reasoning as well as abductive reasoning.
2. In the experiments, different approximate algorithms show significant differences in solving time, despite having almost equivalent time complexity. How can this be explained?
3. In line 80, should it be "less likely"?
reviews.limitations: Yes.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper addresses abductive reasoning tasks such as generating MAP and Marginal MAP (MMAP) explanations in Logical Credal Networks (LCNs). Given that LCNs encode sets of distributions over their interpretations, a complete or partial explanation of the evidence may correspond to multiple distributions. Thus, the authors define the maximin/maximax MAP and MMAP tasks for LCNs as finding complete or partial MAP assignments with maximum lower/upper probability given the evidence. They propose several search algorithms that combine depth-first search, limited-discrepancy search, or simulated annealing with exact evaluations of MAP assignments using marginal inference for LCNs. Additionally, they develop an approximate message-passing scheme and extend limited discrepancy search and simulated annealing to use approximate evaluations of MAP assignments during the search. Experiments show that the approximation schemes which they have proposed can scale to much larger problems compared to search methods.

Strengths:
1. The research is very detailed, providing an excellent formalization of abductive reasoning in LCNs. This formalization addresses a significant gap in previous LCN research, where solving MAP was challenging, and developing corresponding algorithms is not a trivial extension. The authors list several algorithms for solving this and successfully implement and compare them in detail.
2. The approximate algorithms proposed by the authors significantly improve both the solving time and the scale of solvable problems compared to previous search-based methods.

Weaknesses:
The experimental setup is relatively simple, lacking more practical industrial examples and relying more on basic toy experiments. Additionally, there is no comparison with other methods.

Questions:
1. In the initial LCN papers, the authors compared LCN with ProbLog and MLN. Can your method be compared with these? For example, ProbLog is a direct derivative of logic programming, which can perform basic reasoning, including deductive reasoning as well as abductive reasoning.
2. In the experiments, different approximate algorithms show significant differences in solving time, despite having almost equivalent time complexity. How can this be explained?
3. In line 80, should it be "less likely"?

Limitations:
Yes.

Rating:
7
reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

**Summary:**  
This paper makes a significant contribution by addressing abductive reasoning tasks—specifically MAP and Marginal MAP (MMAP) inference—in Logical Credal Networks (LCNs). LCNs encode sets of distributions over interpretations, meaning explanations (MAP assignments) may correspond to multiple distributions. The authors formally define *maximin* and *maximax* variants of MAP/MMAP tasks for LCNs, which seek assignments maximizing the lower or upper probability bounds given evidence (Section 3.1). They propose exact search-based methods (DFS, LDS, SA in Section 3.2) that evaluate assignments via costly marginal inference, alongside scalable approximations: a message-passing scheme (AMAP, Section 3.3) and hybrid search methods (ALDS, ASA, Section 3.4). Experiments on random and realistic LCNs (Section 4) demonstrate that exact methods are limited to small instances (≤8 variables), while approximations handle larger problems (up to 100 variables in Table 3). The paper fills a critical gap in LCN literature, which previously focused solely on marginal inference.

**Strengths:**  
1. **Rigorous Formalization:** The paper provides a meticulous formalization of abductive reasoning in LCNs (Section 3.1), addressing a notable gap. Defining *maximin/maximax* MAP/MMAP tasks is non-trivial, given LCNs' credal semantics, and the authors adeptly adapt concepts from graphical models to this more complex setting.  
2. **Algorithmic Innovation:** The suite of algorithms (Sections 3.2–3.4) is comprehensive. The exact search methods (DFS/LDS/SA) are well-grounded, while the approximate schemes (AMAP/ALDS/ASA) offer practical scalability. AMAP’s extension of ARIEL [18] to MAP tasks (Algorithm 4) is particularly novel, and the hybrid approaches (e.g., ASA initialized with AMAP) intelligently balance efficiency and solution quality (Figure 2).  
3. **Scalability Demonstrations:** Empirical results (Tables 1–3) compellingly validate the approximations’ effectiveness. For example, AMAP solves 100-variable factuality LCNs in ~30 minutes (Table 3), while exact methods time out beyond 8 variables. The ablation of discrepancy limits (Figure 3) and solution quality (Figure 2) adds depth to the analysis.  
4. **Practical Relevance:** The factuality scoring application (Section 4) illustrates real-world utility, linking MMAP inference to LLM verification—a timely contribution given AI safety concerns.  

**Weaknesses:**  
1. **Limited Experimental Scope:** While random and realistic LCNs are used, experiments lack industrial-scale benchmarks or comparisons to non-LCN baselines (e.g., ProbLog, MLNs). The "realistic" LCNs (Table 2) are small (≤10 variables), and the factuality case study (Table 3) uses synthetic data. Broader evaluations (e.g., on standardized abductive reasoning datasets) would strengthen impact.  
2. **Absence of Baselines:** The paper does not compare against existing abductive reasoning tools (e.g., ProbLog’s abduction or MLN MAP solvers). As noted in the introduction, LCNs generalize probabilistic logics like MLNs; a comparison would contextualize the computational trade-offs of handling imprecision.  
3. **Underdeveloped Practical Insights:** The factuality application (Section 4) is promising but under-explored. For instance, how does MMAP-based factuality scoring (e.g., FActScore [24]) compare empirically to heuristic methods?  

**Questions:**  
1. In prior LCN work [11], comparisons were made with ProbLog and MLNs. Can your MAP/MMAP methods be benchmarked against these? ProbLog supports abductive reasoning but assumes precise probabilities—how does its performance or solution quality differ on equivalent tasks?  
2. In Table 1, AMAP, ALDS, and ASA have similar theoretical complexity (O(2^(2^r))), yet AMAP is consistently faster (e.g., 28.42s vs. 3809.23s for n=20). Is this due to implementation overhead in search, or does AMAP benefit from optimized message passing?  
3. In Example 1 (Section 2.1), the sentence "Bronchitis (B) is more likely than Smoking (S)" implies P(B) > P(S). However, the LCN sentence in Figure 1a states "0.3 ≤ P(B) ≤ 0.9" and "0.1 ≤ P(S) ≤ 0.4"—suggesting P(B) ≥ P(S) is possible but not guaranteed. Is this intentional?  

**Limitations:**  
The authors transparently note key limitations:  
- Exact methods (DFS/LDS/SA) are confined to tiny instances (n ≤ 8) due to exponential complexity (Section 4).  
- AMAP assumes proposition independence (Section 3.3), risking local optima; this is mitigated but not eliminated by ALDS/ASA (Figure 2).  
- Factuality LCNs rely on synthetic entailment/contradiction relationships (Section 4), and real-world noise may affect robustness.  

**Conclusion:**  
This paper makes a valuable contribution by formalizing and solving abductive reasoning in LCNs. The proposed algorithms, especially the scalable approximations, enable applications previously infeasible under LCNs’ imprecise semantics. While broader empirical validation and baseline comparisons would strengthen the work, the core innovations in formalization and scalability are significant. The paper merits acceptance for its rigorous technical foundation and practical algorithms.  

**Rating:**  
7 (Good paper, accept)
